summary: Redesign the story spine for Kamikakushi as a grounded Edo folk-mystery (no magic, mediocre-start protagonist); generate diverse spines, synthesize the best, adversarially verify against hard constraints

canonical.title_fit: "Kamikakushi" (神隠し, "hidden by the kami / spirited away") is the word Asagiri valley reaches for whenever a person vanishes — and the valley already has its sacred case: a child named Tama who was "taken by the mountain god" from the Kurosawa goshi estate's lands a decade ago, mourned every Bon with rice and a child's straw sandal left at the mountain shrine. So when a half-drowned, memoryless 17-year-old washes up below the estate after a flood, the village does not see a stranger — it sees the kami GIVING Tama back. The title is the valley's first and fondest lie, and it lies on three layers the game peels in turn: (1) the protagonist was not spirited away but concussed and swept downriver by an ordinary flood; (2) the original Tama was not taken by any god but driven out by human ugliness — a debt, a violent house, and adults who let a comforting spirit-story stand because the truth shamed them; and (3) "hidden by the kami" is, by the end, revealed as the exact phrase powerful men use to bury what they let happen — a drowned upstream hamlet "the gods punished" was in fact a neglected embankment quietly sacrificed to protect tax fields. The same word — for grief, for shame, for negligence dressed as fate — names every mystery in the game, and not one of them is the work of a spirit.
canonical.premise_one_liner: A mediocre 17-year-old farmhand wakes injured and nameless at a remote lower-samurai estate where a grieving valley is certain he is Tama, the child "spirited away by the kami" ten years ago — and through nothing but daily toil and a refusal to quit he must rebuild himself from useless to barely-capable while uncovering the wholly human truths the kamikakushi legend was invented to hide: who he actually is, what really happened to the real Tama, and why the road and river that nearly killed him still run, lethal and lied-about, right past his new home.
canonical.grounded_amnesia_cause: Blunt head trauma plus near-drowning and exposure — all medically real, no spirit involved. A neglected upstream embankment failed on a rain-swollen autumn night and a flash flood tore through the mountain pass and the porters' caravan the protagonist traveled with; he took a rock or beam to the skull, half-drowned, was swept downriver, and snagged at a weir below the Kurosawa estate where a charcoal-burner found him at dawn. The resulting retrograde amnesia is the ordinary concussion-plus-hypoxia-plus-fever kind: he keeps language and bodily habits (he ties a porter's load-knot without thinking) but has lost his autobiographical past, with the days before blurred by fever. The estate herbalist dresses a healing scalp gash that physically grounds the injury, and memory returns never in a magic flash but in slow mundane sensory fragments — paddy-mud smell, a familiar road, a name surfacing in a recurring half-drowning dream — and only ever restores an ordinary, sad past, never any hidden skill.
canonical.central_mystery: Four nested human questions, deliberately tangled so the valley's supernatural certainty is the red herring blocking every mundane answer. (1) AM I TAMA? — the village insists the kami returned their lost child, and the player is driven to learn whether the face the grieving see is really his. (2) IF NOT, WHO AM I? — the calluses, the porter's knots, the name in the water demand an origin. (3) THEN WHAT REALLY HAPPENED TO THE REAL TAMA? — if he isn't the spirited-away child, the original disappearance reopens as an unsolved human case. (4) WHY DOES THE VALLEY (AND THE POWER ABOVE IT) NEED ALL OF THIS BURIED? — the same flood, road, and officials that nearly killed him are still moving rice and silver and still calling their crimes "the will of the kami." The pull is that the answers are not a distant quest but physically nearby and tightening: the more competent and curious he becomes, the more he becomes a problem to people who liked him better as a ghost.
canonical.grounded_resolution: He is NOT Tama. The resemblance is real but coincidental and suggestible — right age, similar build, a birthmark near the same place — and the people who "recognize" him are remembering a seven-year-old through a decade of grief and guilt that did the rest (the legend has even drifted on details, a fair-play clue). His true identity, recovered piece by mundane piece: Ren, an orphaned tenant-farmhand-turned-porter from Kuzuhara, an upstream hamlet, indentured to a transport guild to work off a dead parent's debt. Kuzuhara did not "anger the gods." During a famine year the district deputy magistrate's office, pressed for its rice quota, quietly diverted the corvée labor and relief-rice that maintained the upstream embankment to protect the well-kept downstream dykes guarding the tax fields and its own interests; a frightened local headman doctored the ledgers to cover the shortfall. When the monsoon came, the undermined seki failed in the night and the poorest, lowest hamlet drowned while the protected fields held — negligence shading into a chosen sacrifice of the expendable, then blamed on the kami and on an "unlucky, impious" village. Ren was the porter on a guild caravan caught in that same flood; the guild wrote him off because the route was running short-weighted tax rice and smuggled silver, and admitting the man meant admitting the contraband. The REAL Tama, meanwhile, was never taken by anything: Tama (a girl, not the boy the legend "remembers" — the gender drift is the clue) ran from a violent stepfather and a household that had agreed to sell her into service to clear a rice debt owed to the Kurosawa estate; a midwife and a traveling peddler helped her flee down-valley, and she has been alive and grown the whole decade, working in a post-town, too afraid and too ashamed to return. The adults who knew let "the kami took her" stand because a tragic miracle is easier to live with than "we were about to sell a child." The estate is bound into it too: the rice debt that nearly sold Tama is the same debt-and-quota machine that drowned Kuzuhara, which is why the estate's own old guilt and the magistrate's racket are one rotten root. RESOLUTION is human and partial — never vengeance-magic, never a hero's clean win. Ren can undo none of it. He survives, recovers his ordinary sad past, finds the few scattered survivors (chiefly a fellow Kuzuhara survivor), reunites the valley with the living, grown Tama, and forces the buried truths into the open through evidence and testimony — a doctored ledger, a surviving corvée tally-stick, a guilt-sick clerk, the doctored masu measuring-box, a porter-witness. The reachable culprits answer (the headman, the steward, the enforcer); the truly untouchable magistrate largely escapes, because that is how the world is — but Kuzuhara's drowned are named at last, Tama is freed to choose, and Ren claims a true name and a life he built by hand instead of the borrowed miracle he was handed.
canonical.folklore_role: Folklore is the valley's emotional weather and its conscience-cover — never its physics, always load-bearing as atmosphere and as a lens on human guilt, and always treated with respect rather than mockery (it is how frightened, powerless, grieving people make the unbearable bearable). The kamikakushi belief is the master lie: it explains the protagonist, absolves the adults who failed Tama, and lets the magistrate's office reframe negligence as "the kami's punishment." Around it the valley breathes superstition the player constantly hears as rumor, ritual, and gossip, then watches dissolve into human cause: a roadside jizo and a spirit-gate shimenawa where Tama was "last seen"; the yearly soul-calling rite for the spirited-away; offerings at the boundary-stone where Ren was found; a tanuki blamed for rice that is actually being skimmed; a kappa at the weir/ford where bodies surface (a real undertow, a poacher's weighted nets, and the smugglers' sinking-spot for evidence and inconvenient people); fox-fire on the ridge (a charcoal-burner's hidden kiln, and once a lantern carried to a grave); a one-eyed mountain god on the high pass (a scarred lookout watching the smuggling route); a yamanba (a widow burned out by the same flood, surviving feral); a tengu of the high cedars (a fugitive ex-foot-soldier). Each belief is true to the heart that holds it and false to the world, and the game lets the player FEEL the dread before it shows the cause. A deliberately thin margin of ambiguity is kept for unease and never resolved or mechanized — a fox that twice seems to lead him to safety, one omen at the shrine that lines up too neatly, a single offering at the boundary-stone left by a hand he never identifies — enough to keep the night woods uneasy, never enough to confirm the supernatural, and never granting him any power.
canonical.mediocre_start_honored: He is plainly, mechanically ordinary from the first minute, and the game says so in text and numbers. The opening "gameplay" is literally convalescence — resting, drinking water, being spoon-fed millet gruel — because he is a concussed, feverish invalid who can barely stand. When he can work, his hands blister on the first hoe; he is slower than the ten-year-old who shows him the paddy; the steward calls him flatly "another mouth, soft and clumsy, eats more than he earns"; a guard sneers at the idea of him holding a weapon. His starting stats are low and his early labor is slow and tiring, with small yields and an aching back. Critically, there is NO hidden edge to discover: no bloodline, no secret training, no sword that answers to him, no body that "remembers" combat. Every returning memory is of ordinary hard labor and ordinary fear — a poor porter's life, not a warrior's. When he ties a load-knot on instinct the carter says only "huh, you've hauled before," explicitly a laborer's habit, not talent. The single thing the village treats as special about him — that he is the returned Tama — is FALSE, a projection of others' grief. His one genuine asset is not skill but temperament: stubbornness, a strong back, a patient temper, and a refusal to stop getting up — exactly the traits a good laborer needs and exactly what the incremental grind rewards.
canonical.growth_through_effort: Every number that goes up is sweat the player chose to spend, and the story names it relentlessly. The incremental economy IS the training montage: stamina, strength, a calloused-hands stat, and skill ranks (Hoeing, Foraging, Woodcutting, Fishing, Carrying, later Spear/Staff) rise only through thousands of repetitions, with diminishing returns you push past only by showing up daily and never quitting — the literal zero-to-competent arc. Combat capacity is gated behind labor-built conditioning: you cannot stand against a bandit until months of hauling rice bales have given you a back and wind, and the spear is taught as "just a long hoe held with intent." The mentor's creed, stated early and paid off in the finale: "Talent is a story the lucky tell. You are not lucky. So you will work." Growth is grind, not gift, and the game keeps it diegetic: plateaus are real, the answer to a wall is never a magic item but more reps, better technique, more rest, and better tools you crafted; overtraining injuries force rest, reinforcing that the body earns this slowly. A "kept at it" thread rewards failing-and-repeating a task enough times with a breakthrough, dramatizing perseverance over innate ability. Even the mystery is bought with grind — village trust (a tracked reputation earned by completed labor and survived seasons) is what unlocks the confessions that crack the case. By endgame he is genuinely capable, but the game is careful to show it took a year of dawns, so the player feels they BUILT this person rather than discovered a chosen one — and the meta-progress that survives the late-game reset is exactly this hard-won competence and standing, proof that perseverance compounds where talent and the supernatural never came.
canonical.combat_framing: Early enemies are mundane, grounded countryside threats — a boar wrecking the millet, monkeys raiding stores, a fox in the henhouse, a rabid stray-dog pack, leeches/hornets/a viper as hazards — escalating to human danger (a lone desperate rice-thief, then famine-driven bandits of ronin gone to brigandage) and the genuinely lethal mountains (cold, falls, fog, hunger-maddened animals), with every 'yokai' just one of these wearing folklore's costume. THE HUMBLING FIRST FIGHT: weeks in, cornered on the wood-gathering trail or defending the grain store, he swings a carrying-pole or the estate's hatchet, lands at most one lucky blow, and is then thrashed — disarmed, ribs cracked, robbed of the tool, left in the dirt — surviving only by luck or because Jūbei arrives, never by skill. The game states the lesson outright ('you swing like you're chasing crows from the rice; the blade is heavier than any hoe, and he is faster and stronger and trained, and you are not'), and the shame of limping home to confess he lost the estate's tool is the exact engine that drives him to beg for drills. No fight before that point suggests competence beyond a scared farm boy flailing; combat stays idle/auto-resolving afterward, but its outcomes are explicitly a function of the conditioning and skill ground out through labor and training — for a long time the survivable play is to avoid, flee, or fight in numbers, and he wins later only because he trained.
canonical.yokai_handling: Each named yokai is introduced as genuine, respected village dread, investigated as a quest line through ordinary work, and resolved to a concrete human or natural cause — but the game lingers in the unease before resolving, and the reveal is delivered with restraint so the world keeps its hush rather than collapsing into a Scooby-Doo unmasking. The enforced one-to-one belief→cause table: the weir/ford KAPPA = a real undertow and undercut bank, a poacher's weighted nets, AND the smugglers' sinking-spot for evidence and drowned witnesses (the player physically recovers a weighted bundle and, once, a corpse from the pool). The TANUKI stealing rice = a skimming foreman feeding the racket. FOX-FIRE / ghost-lights on the ridge = a charcoal-burner's hidden kiln and, later, men of the magistrate moving through forbidden ground at night. The ONE-EYED MOUNTAIN GOD on the pass = the scarred ronin enforcer's lookout. The YAMANBA of the high woods = a flood-burned widow surviving feral, "defeated" by being fed and resettled (a mercy quest, not a kill). The TENGU of the high cedars = a fugitive ex-foot-soldier who becomes a wary teacher of mountain-craft and a key witness. The "night the road walks" / spirited-away = landslide-prone, fog-blind terrain where travelers genuinely die — exactly how Ren arrived — plus people who fled debt, conscription, violence, or shame and were too ashamed or too dead to return. The yearly SOUL-CALLING rite = grief-work that nonetheless yields hard evidence (the priest's register of every vanished traveler ties the disappearances to the road). The retained ambiguity is atmospheric only and never mechanical: a recurring half-drowning dream of a voice calling his name (it turns out to be a real survivor, not a spirit); the twice-helpful fox; one shrine omen too neat; an unidentified hand at the boundary-stone — the player is allowed a shiver, never a spell, and no yokai is ever shown to be real or to grant anything.

act_structure.act: Act 0 — The Weir (Prologue / onboarding)
act_structure.summary: He wakes feverish and nameless in a storehouse on the Kurosawa goshi estate, pulled half-drowned from the river with a dressed head wound and hands that know labor he can't remember learning. The steward's daughter Sayo names him 'Tama' on sight, and word races through Asagiri-mura that the kami have returned the spirited-away child. UI is near-bare and diegetic: a body/rest bar, a rice counter, one or two survival tasks (drink, rest, then haul water, pull weeds) — because he is an invalid earning his keep. Seeds: the first intrusive half-drowning dream (a voice, a name, dark water), and the gentle wrongness of a whole village treating a stranger as a miracle while he can barely lift a hoe. Ends when he survives his first task and earns a sleeping-place — he has CHOSEN to stay and find out who he is.

act_structure.act: Act 1 — Earning Your Rice (the labor economy unlocks)
act_structure.summary: Recovery through repetition. Season by season he becomes a trusted hand as farming, then foraging, woodcutting, fishing, and basic crafting unlock one panel at a time — each gated diegetically by rising stamina and by NPCs literally granting access (the woodlot, permission to fish the estate stretch). The rice/koku economy comes online; numbers climb slowly and visibly earned. Folklore texture thickens: the soul-calling rite, the feared ford, the tanuki blamed for missing rice, offerings at the stone where he was found. He grows attached to the estate, to old Jūbei and the herbalist, and especially to Sayo — whose desperate hope that he IS her lost childhood friend is the warm hook and the engine of the red herring. Threads: a recurring name in the dream, flashes of paddies that aren't Asagiri's, the off-limits 'cursed' upstream valley no one will discuss. Act ends with the humbling near-fatal fight.

act_structure.act: Act 2 — Calluses (training and the first threads)
act_structure.summary: Shamed by the beating, he begs aging guardsman Jūbei for drills; the training/combat/conditioning systems unlock as a grind loop he keeps failing, and idle auto-combat against vermin and the mountain's lesser dangers opens — all gated behind labor-built stamina. Investigating the thieves and the 'tanuki' leads to a skimming foreman and the first hard fact: the road through the valley moves more rice than the books admit. He meets the grounded 'yokai' as fearsome rumor (kappa, one-eyed god, tengu). A recovered manifest scrap, a porter's-knot habit, and a debt-token surface; the name in the dream resolves to 'Kuzuhara,' the drowned upstream hamlet no one will name. The deputy magistrate's man takes a too-keen interest in the 'spirited-away boy,' and the legend starts not adding up — people 'remember' different ages, even a different gender. The mystery sharpens from 'who am I' to 'what happened up the valley.'

act_structure.act: Act 3 — The Things People Remember (investigation and the mountains)
act_structure.summary: Now capable enough to range, he pushes into the forbidden upstream ruins and the dangerous mountains, unlocking deep gathering, expeditions/logistics, and an investigation layer. The 'haunting' of the dead valley resolves into a feral survivor, Kiku, who recognizes him — both were Kuzuhara children. Together they uncover the disaster's shape: the broken seki, the bones, the burned relief-storehouse — and the darker turn, that the embankment was neglected and the failure covered. The kappa-ford resolves (weighted evidence, a corpse), the one-eyed god resolves (the enforcer's lookout). Meanwhile the legend collapses fairly: he finds proof Tama was a girl who RAN, meaning he was never her — the gut-punch that detonates the village's miracle. His own fragments confirm an ordinary porter's past. The two buried lies — the estate's old debt-and-near-sale of Tama, and the magistrate's drowned hamlet — reveal themselves as one rotten root: the same rice-quota machine.

act_structure.act: Act 4 — The Ledger and Your Own Name (climax, the case)
act_structure.summary: Not a magic duel but a case, against power. He tracks the threads down-valley to the peddler, the post-town, and finally the living, grown Tama (now 'Oharu'), who fears the village that almost sold her; and he confirms his own identity as Ren, the porter the guild wrote off. With Kiku and allies he assembles mundane proof — the doctored corvée-and-relief ledger, a surviving tally-stick, the doctored masu measuring-box, a guilt-sick junior clerk's testimony, the priest's register, a porter-witness. Pressure peaks: the powerful want the valley to stay 'cursed' and forgotten and now hunt him as a troublesome witness; the estate's own complicity (the rice debt) and reputation are threatened. He still cannot win by force — proven again in a losing skirmish with the scarred enforcer — only by getting the truth above the guilty via formal petition (osso) to a touring inspector/the domain. A grounded, hard-won confrontation resolves it: the reachable culprits (headman, steward, enforcer) answer; the untouchable magistrate largely escapes; Kuzuhara's dead are named and shrined; Tama is freed to choose return or peace. He learns his family did not survive — and that there was never anything heroic to go back to.

act_structure.act: Act 5 — Spring Again (diegetic reset / new cycle)
act_structure.summary: A grounded season-cycle time-skip, not reincarnation or a loop. The valley is safer but unguarded and scarred, and Jūbei is too old to drill the farm boys or walk the trails. Ren — no longer a nameless invalid but a known, capable young man — is given standing of his own: he accepts stewardship of the estate's fields and mountain trails, or sets out with Kiku (and the tengu-witness Genza, and Oharu if she chooses) to re-found Kuzuhara as a tiny resettlement and open a humble training-and-workhouse for other famine orphans and displaced strays. The calendar turns to spring; the cramped estate-tenant UI and its early caps are left behind, the map and economy expand to a higher tier, and ALL hard-won skill, reputation, recipes, tools, and relationships carry forward as meta-progress and as the baseline he now TEACHES to NPC students (who become idle producers/fighters). The fantasy of effort over talent becomes literally generational and mechanical — a man who climbed once now building the ladder for others, starting them from exactly the zero he started at. New, farther-off kamikakushi rumors drift up from distant valleys, seeding the next cycle's mysteries on the same grounded promise.

canonical.diegetic_reset: A grounded season-cycle time-skip framed as the veteran starting a bigger venture — never reincarnation, never a magic loop. After the case breaks, a full year turns and Ren, now trusted and skilled, is formally given a role of his own: aging Jūbei hands him stewardship of the Kurosawa estate's fields and mountain trails, OR (player-chosen, both grounded) he sets out with Kiku to re-found drowned Kuzuhara as a tiny resettlement and open a small training-and-workhouse for other strays and famine orphans, teaching them the same survive-by-effort skills he ground out. Mechanically this is the meta-progress carry-over: the cramped estate-tenant UI and its early caps are left behind; the map, season, and economy reset fresh to spring planting at a HIGHER tier; but every hard-won skill rank, reputation point, recipe, tool, koku stockpile, and relationship persists as the new starting baseline — and now also as bonuses he TEACHES to NPC students, who themselves become idle producers and fighters (a management/teaching layer). It reads as the natural Edo rhythm of seasons and of an apprentice becoming a master: hard-won competence compounding onto firmer ground, the loop reopening one tier up so the numbers never run dry, and the thematic spine — effort over talent — made literally generational as the climber builds the ladder for others starting from the same zero he did. New, farther-off kamikakushi rumors seed the next cycle's mystery on the identical grounded promise.

key_npcs.name: Jūbei
key_npcs.role: Aging lower-samurai retainer / lone guardsman of the Kurosawa estate — a competent-but-never-great old foot-soldier with bad knees, who knows he never saw real glory
key_npcs.function: Mentor and combat/training-systems gatekeeper, and the embodiment of the no-talent / all-effort theme. Saves or fails to save the protagonist in the humbling first fight, then runs the brutal failing-forward drill loop; refuses to train him until labor and that defeat have earned it. Speaks the creed 'Talent is a story the lucky tell — so you will work.' Quietly carries famine-era guilt (his estate's fields held while Kuzuhara drowned), giving him a personal stake in the truth; his decline motivates the founding of the training hall at the reset.

key_npcs.name: Sayo
key_npcs.role: The steward's daughter, ~16, sharp, kind, and the keeper of the valley's informal news
key_npcs.function: Guide, emotional anchor, and the living heart of the red herring. She names the protagonist 'Tama' and needs most desperately for him to be her lost childhood friend, so her hope is the engine that keeps everyone — and the player — from seeing the mundane truth. She tutorializes the farm/economy loop, gates early reputation, and channels rumor-as-gameplay (which yokai gossip points to which real crime). Her grief and her eventual reckoning with the truth — including her own family's complicity in the rice debt — are the emotional spine; she is the person the truth will most hurt and most free.

key_npcs.name: Obaa Sato
key_npcs.role: Village herbalist and wise-woman who dresses the protagonist's head wound
key_npcs.function: Grounding voice and lore-keeper. Her treatment of the scalp gash physically anchors the mundane amnesia and lets her name his symptoms (concussion, fever) rather than visions; she narrates folk-belief sympathetically while privately knowing it is coping, gently debunks superstition, and dispenses early survival/foraging/crafting knowledge. The compassionate human face of the village's denial, and the warm relationship whose quiet faith that he is 'just a hurt boy' anchors the human core.

key_npcs.name: Kiku
key_npcs.role: A feral survivor living in the ruins of the drowned upstream valley
key_npcs.function: Mystery-key, co-investigator, and found-family anchor. The 'vengeful spirit' of the cursed valley resolved into a person — a fellow Kuzuhara child who knew Ren before and holds the memory he lost and the will for justice he must earn. Recognizing him is the hinge that converts the legend into a human case; she becomes co-founder of the resettlement at the diegetic reset, making the found-family tangible.

key_npcs.name: Genza (the 'tengu')
key_npcs.role: A fugitive ex-foot-soldier / deserter living rough in the high cedars, feared as the tengu of the pass
key_npcs.function: Grounded-yokai reveal, mountain-craft teacher, and key witness in one beat (cast-efficiency by design). Becomes a wary mentor in tracking and mountain survival once the player earns his trust, and holds evidence about who really used the smuggling pass — bridging the legend and the racket. A second, gentler model of the effort theme: a man who survives on hard-learned skill, not gift.

key_npcs.name: Kuroiwa
key_npcs.role: The district deputy magistrate's agent (tedai/daikan's man) — outwardly lawful, polite, patient, and dangerous, the local architect of the tax-quota cover-up and smuggling racket
key_npcs.function: Primary human antagonist: the power that wants the kamikakushi story to stick. He benefits from the comforting myth and tightens the noose as the protagonist grows curious, eventually hunting him as a witness. Defeated by evidence and testimony, not violence — and crucially the truly untouchable magistrate above him largely escapes, keeping the resolution honest about power.

key_npcs.name: Headman Yagōemon
key_npcs.role: The district village headman (shōya/nanushi) who doctored the corvée-and-relief ledgers and let the 'cursed village' story reach the magistrate
key_npcs.function: Antagonist-of-record and the humanly-reachable culprit — not a monster but a frightened middle-man who chose his own village and his own neck over Kuzuhara, and who let the comfortable silence stand because the truth implicates him and the estate in nearly selling a child. The social obstacle of the late acts; the face Ren can actually force to answer.

key_npcs.name: Hanzaki
key_npcs.role: A masterless swordsman (ronin) enforcer, scarred over one eye — the man behind the 'one-eyed mountain god' rumor and the physical muscle of the racket
key_npcs.function: The recurring physical threat and the thematic MIRROR: a man of real, natural talent gone rotten, set against the protagonist's earned, ordinary competence. He out-fights Ren every early encounter; the eventual confrontation is survived not by out-talenting him but by trained endurance and everything ground out over a year — proving the game's thesis in the body.

key_npcs.name: Clerk Naozane
key_npcs.role: A guilt-sick junior clerk in the magistrate's office who transcribed the lies
key_npcs.function: Mystery-key and witness — the crack in the cover-up. He can't live with what he wrote and supplies the documentary proof and testimony that make the petition (osso) possible; the grounded 'key' that replaces any magical revelation, and a quiet study in conscience versus self-preservation.

key_npcs.name: Oharu (the real Tama)
key_npcs.role: The 'spirited-away' child, alive and grown, working in a down-valley post-town, terrified of the home that almost sold her
key_npcs.function: Mystery payoff and living proof. Her existence detonates the red herring and is the destination of the late-game expedition lines; reuniting/reconciling her with the valley is the climax's human heart. Deliberately costly and incomplete — she may not forgive the valley, and some adults are never absolved — keeping the ending bittersweet rather than fairy-tale.

key_npcs.name: The peddler Sōkichi
key_npcs.role: An itinerant medicine-seller who travels the whole region
key_npcs.function: Quest-giver and information broker — the practical engine that lets the player chase clues beyond the home village. He trades rumor, rare goods, and the down-valley trail of evidence that leads to Oharu, and is a grounded source of many 'yokai' tales (each of which he can explain if pressed).

canonical.emotional_hook: The ache of being handed an identity you didn't earn and can't be sure you want — and the harder dignity of becoming someone real anyway, by your own hands. A whole grieving valley NEEDS the protagonist to be their lost child; their guilt and hope are pinned to his face, and the kindest people — Sayo above all — will be hurt by the truth. So the player feels two pulls at once: the human longing to belong, to simply BE the miracle everyone wants and slip into a ready-made home; and the harder, truer dignity of refusing the borrowed name and building a real self callus by callus. The growth-through-toil loop makes that dignity tangible — every number that climbs is proof he is constructing an honest self rather than wearing a dead child's. Beneath it runs grief with no object (hands that remember a family he can't picture, a name in the water he can't place) and the refusal to let the drowned be erased twice — first by the flood, then by the comforting lie. The catharsis is never power: it is a nobody teenager becoming the person who lets a grieving sister mourn honestly, who reunites a frightened woman with the home that wronged her, and who makes a forgotten village's name be spoken aloud — and his own forgettable past landing as bittersweet relief, because he was never going to be saved by destiny, only by his own refusal to stop and by the people who chose to keep him. It honors the player's literal investment: the hours of grind ARE the character's hard-won belonging, so the numbers going up feel like a person growing roots.
canonical.how_it_paces_unlocks: Story access and UI unlocks are the same currency — trust and competence, both bought with labor — so the incremental reveal is fully diegetic and the numbers-going-up advances in lockstep with the truth-coming-out. Act 0 keeps the screen near-empty (body/rest bar, rice counter, one or two tasks) because the character is a bedridden invalid; recovery IS the tutorial, justifying the minimal UI. Surviving the first harvest unlocks the core peaceful economy panel by panel (farm → forage → woodcut → fish → craft → koku), each tied diegetically to rising stamina and to NPCs literally granting access (the woodlot key, permission to fish the estate stretch). The humbling first defeat is the gate that unlocks the entire training and idle-combat suite — explicitly because the story has just proven he NEEDS it, so the player wants the new buttons rather than being dumped into them, and it stays grind-gated so the climb is felt. Rising reputation (earned purely by sustained labor and survived seasons) is the key that unlocks NPC confessions, expedition routes, and craft tiers — i.e., mystery progress is literally purchased with grind, marrying curiosity to the loop. Investigation gates exploration: each debunked folk-belief opens a new, more dangerous area (the ford, then the mountains and forbidden upstream ruins, then the pass), which in turn unlocks deeper gathering, expedition logistics, and tougher idle combat. The conspiracy gates an evidence/influence layer (gathering proof, swaying witnesses, the petition) and deeper crafting/economy, because solving the case requires resources and allies. To keep curiosity from going cold between big reputation-gated confessions, small story fragments drip on a steady cadence (sensory memory flashes, peddler rumors, dream-name beats) tied to concrete thresholds (koku totals, skill tiers, area unlocks). The Act 5 time-skip then re-gates a whole new valley-tier of systems plus a teaching/management layer behind the veteran's standing, restarting the unlock cadence at a higher floor so the incremental UI has a fresh, larger canvas to bloom across again.
risks: Mystery payoff could underwhelm if the racket reads as a stock 'corrupt official runs smuggling' plot; the freshness must come from the SPECIFIC human textures braided in — the mistaken-identity legend, the gender/age drift as fair clues, the rigged masu and corvée tally-stick, the estate's own complicity, and especially the partial/bitter justice — not from the premise.

Mistaken-identity reveal ('you were never Tama') and the deflating-own-past beat ('just an indentured porter') can feel like a cheat or anticlimax; plant fair, re-readable clues early (age/gender drift, porter's-knot habit, debt-token, the dream-name) so the twist feels earned, and make the chosen-name resolution emotionally satisfying regardless.

Tonal whiplash between cozy Edo labor and grim material (a drowned village, a near-sold child, official negligence, violence) — needs deliberate modulation, off-screen restraint for the darkest beats, and a warm daily-loop and found-family counterweight (Sayo, Obaa Sato, Kiku, Jūbei) so the dark turns reinforce rather than drown the peaceful texture the genre needs.

The no-magic rule in a folklore setting risks the Scooby-Doo anticlimax; enforce a one-to-one belief→cause table for every yokai, debunk with dawning dread rather than gotcha unmaskings, and keep the thin residual ambiguity rare, off-screen, mundane-readable, and never mechanized.

Honoring the mediocre start fights the genre's fast-dopamine instinct; if early grind is too punishing players quit before payoff, so the humbling must read as motivating not demoralizing, and the peaceful economy must be generously rewarding within each act even before the next narrative unlock.

Mystery-vs-grind pacing: gating reveals purely behind long grinds can stall the story; drip small fragments (memory flashes, peddler rumors, dream beats) on a steady cadence between big reputation-gated confessions, and tie every beat to a concrete reachable threshold.

The justice resolution must land as deliberate PARTIAL justice (reachable culprits answer, the untouchable magistrate mostly escapes, Kuzuhara is named) — too clean a villain-kill betrays the grounded 'power escapes / no hero' truth, while total failure deflates the player; thread it carefully.

Antagonist nuance: Kuroiwa, Yagōemon, the steward, and even the estate must read as flawed humans (fear, pride, loyalty-to-the-lie, self-preservation) rather than mustache-twirlers, or the grounded-tragedy thesis collapses into melodrama; Hanzaki specifically must function as the talent-gone-rotten mirror, not a cartoon.
The real Tama (Oharu) being alive risks a tidy sentimental ending; keep the reunion costly and incomplete (she may not forgive, some adults are never absolved) to preserve grounded melancholy.

Cast bloat — many NPCs carry both a yokai-debunk and a mystery clue; enforce double-duty (Genza, Kiku, Sōkichi, the clerk each do their reveal and their clue in the same beat) so the web stays legible.

The diegetic reset (founding a workhouse, teaching from zero) could feel like a grind-reset punishment rather than a reward if meta-progress and the 'building the ladder for others' framing aren't made tangible and satisfying; the teaching/management layer must be a clear power-up, not a soft reincarnation that muddies the no-magic promise.

Edo-period authenticity (goshi status, daikan/tedai administration, osso peasant petitions and their real danger, corvée labor and seki upkeep, kaido porter culture, masu measures, famine relief) must be researched enough to feel real without becoming a history lecture that stalls gameplay.

weaknesses: Constraint 2 (residual ambiguity) is the riskiest spot, not because anything is confirmed-magical but because three separate ambiguity tokens are stacked: (a) the twice-helpful fox, (b) the one shrine omen 'too neat,' and (c) the unidentified hand leaving an offering at the boundary-stone. Individually each is mundane-readable; together, if a player meets all three unresolved, the cumulative weight can tip 'unease' into 'the game is winking that magic is real.' Recommend the design explicitly mark at most one of these as ever surfacing per playthrough, OR give each a quietly available mundane explanation in the codex so a curious player can deflate it.

The fox-that-twice-leads-him-to-safety is the single most genre-coded supernatural cliche in the list. 'A fox guides the lost traveler' reads as kitsune folklore-made-real far more strongly than the other tokens. It needs the most aggressive mundane grounding (e.g., the fox is following him for dropped food / a den near the only safe path / it is fleeing the same predator he is), or it should be cut, because it is the one item most likely to be read by players as confirmed supernatural aid.

The recurring half-drowning dream 'a voice calling his name' that 'turns out to be a real survivor' is good, but the spine should be explicit that the dream is reconstructed memory/auditory association, not precognition or a psychic link to Kiku — otherwise an attentive player could read the dream as a supernatural tether to a living person, which brushes the no-magic line.

Constraint 3 risk: the 'porter's load-knot tied without thinking' is repeated as charming colour several times. It is correctly framed as a laborer's habit (carter: 'huh, you've hauled before') and explicitly NOT talent. But it is the one beat closest to 'his body remembers a skill,' so it must never be mechanized into a starting stat bonus or a combat carryover. As written it is safe; flag it only so implementation doesn't accidentally convert flavor into a hidden edge.

Constraint 3 risk: Hanzaki is framed as 'real, natural talent gone rotten' as a deliberate thematic mirror. This is fine for an NPC, but it slightly normalizes 'innate talent exists in this world' which could invite a player to wonder if the protagonist has latent talent too. The spine already guards this ('Talent is a story the lucky tell'), but the mirror should be kept unambiguous: Hanzaki's edge should also be shown as partly trained/experienced, not purely god-given, so the world's thesis ('effort, not gift') is never undercut by a character who proves talent is decisive.

Constraint 5: the diegetic reset is grounded and correct, but it is player-branching (steward of the estate OR re-found Kuzuhara). Two reset framings doubles the authenticity and balance surface area and risks one branch feeling like the 'real' ending and the other a consolation. Not a constraint violation, but a scope/clarity weakness worth noting.

Constraint 6 / pacing: 'mystery progress is literally purchased with grind' is thematically elegant but is the spine's biggest play-feel risk — if confessions/clues are hard-gated behind long reputation grinds, the story can stall for hours. The spine acknowledges this (drip cadence) but the safeguard is described, not specified; needs concrete max-gap thresholds so curiosity never goes cold.

Tonal load: a drowned hamlet of the poor deliberately sacrificed, a child nearly sold, feral survivors, official murder of witnesses. This is heavy material for a cozy incremental's daily texture. The spine flags this risk well, but the darkest beats (the deliberate sacrifice of Kuzuhara's poor, drowned witnesses in the kappa-pool) need disciplined off-screen restraint or they will overpower the farm-sim warmth the genre needs.
fixes: Ambiguity budget: pick exactly ONE residual-ambiguity token to ever leave unresolved in a given playthrough (recommend the unidentified-hand offering, the most mundane-readable). Give the fox and the shrine-omen quiet, optional mundane explanations the player can discover, so the night woods stay uneasy without the game appearing to confirm the supernatural.

Re-ground or cut the guiding fox: rewrite it so the fox's two 'rescues' are transparently self-interested animal behavior (scavenging his trail food, denning by the only dry ledge, bolting from the same boar that would have killed him), making it re-readable as coincidence rather than kitsune aid.

Add one line to grounded_amnesia_cause / yokai_handling making the half-drowning dream explicitly reconstructed sensory memory and association — a real voice he heard in the water (Kiku's), recalled, not a vision or a living-link — so it can never be read as precognition.

Lock 'load-knot' and any returning-memory beats as flavor-only at the systems level: add an explicit note that NO returning memory or instinctive habit ever grants a starting stat, recipe, or combat bonus — every number still starts at the bottom and is earned. (The spine says this; make it an implementation guardrail.)

Tune Hanzaki so his superiority is shown as veteran skill + brutality + better tools and conditioning, not pure innate gift; let one line acknowledge he too 'drilled for years,' so the talent-mirror cannot be read as proof that innate talent decides fights in this world.

Specify the drip cadence concretely: define a maximum number of in-game days / a koku-or-skill threshold between guaranteed story fragments, and ensure at least one always-available, non-reputation-gated investigation thread exists, so mystery progress never fully stalls behind grind.

For the reset, designate one branch as the canonical default (recommend re-founding Kuzuhara, which best pays off the 'name the drowned / build the ladder' theme) and frame the estate-stewardship branch as an equally valid alternative, so neither reads as a lesser ending; ensure meta-progress carry-over is identical across branches.

Keep the darkest beats off-screen and reported (testimony, ledgers, a survivor's account) rather than depicted, and protect the cozy daily loop as the dominant minute-to-minute texture so the grim revelations land as weight, not as the game's mood.
authenticity_notes: goshi (郷士 / rural lower-samurai) holding an estate, working its own fields, and extending rice debt to tenants is plausible for the Edo period, though goshi status and wealth varied widely by domain (notably Satsuma/Tosa had large goshi classes). The spine's modest, hands-on estate with a single aging retainer (Jūbei) is a defensible and authentic portrayal; just keep the estate small and not lordly.

daikan/tedai (代官・手代) district administration and a deputy-magistrate's agent running a tax-quota cover-up is historically grounded; tax pressure, quota shortfalls, and falsified village ledgers (a shoya/nanushi doctoring records) are well-attested abuses. Sound.

osso (越訴) — direct/over-the-head peasant petition — is a real and genuinely dangerous act; petitioners risked execution even when grievances were upheld. The spine correctly treats it as perilous and notes the untouchable magistrate largely escapes. This is the single most authenticity-sensitive mechanic; portray the danger honestly (petitioners punished even when right) rather than as a clean legal win.

corvée (kuniyaku/buyaku) labor and seki (堰, weir/embankment) upkeep being diverted from a poor upstream hamlet to protect tax fields is plausible and resonates with real Edo flood-control politics, where downstream/well-connected interests were favored. Strong, grounded core.

masu (枡) measuring-box fraud and a rigged/short measure as evidence is authentic — measure standardization (the shogunate's kyo-masu) and cheating on it were real points of conflict. Good concrete artifact.

kamikakushi (神隠し) as a folk explanation for vanished children/travelers is well-documented and the spine treats it respectfully as grief-coping and cover, not mockery — appropriate and culturally sensitive.

Sensitivity check on Kiku the 'feral survivor' and the yamanba-widow 'surviving feral': avoid dehumanizing 'wild person' tropes; portray them as traumatized survivors of disaster/poverty with dignity and agency, not as savages to be tamed. The 'mercy quest, not a kill' framing for the yamanba is good; extend the same dignity to Kiku.

Bon (お盆) offerings, jizo, shimenawa/spirit-gates, soul-calling rites, and a priest's register of vanished travelers are all period-plausible folk-religious texture; the syncretic mix of kami-belief and Buddhist mourning is accurate for rural Edo. Keep ritual detail respectful and specific rather than generic 'Shinto.'

Indentured porter working off a dead parent's debt to a transport guild, kaido porter/caravan culture, smuggled silver on tax-rice routes — all plausible Edo economic textures; just note inter-domain movement and checkpoints (sekisho) were controlled, so the protagonist's downriver displacement by flood (not free travel) is the right, authentic mechanism for crossing into the valley.

Naming/honorifics: names (Jubei, Sayo, Obaa Sato, Yagoemon, etc.) read period-plausible; ensure status-appropriate address (a goshi household vs. tenants vs. a daikan's agent) is observed, as Edo speech was sharply status-marked — a low authenticity win if dialogue honors it.

Famine + relief-rice diversion + drowned poorest hamlet echoes real Tokugawa famine episodes (Tenmei/Tenpo); keep it generic-rural rather than pinned to a real named disaster/place to avoid misrepresenting a specific historical tragedy.
verify.verdict: PASS. The spine honors all six hard constraints with unusual discipline. The amnesia is mundane (flood + blunt trauma + near-drowning + fever), the protagonist is genuinely and mechanically mediocre with no hidden edge (the one returning-habit beat is explicitly a laborer's, not a warrior's), every yokai resolves one-to-one to a human/natural cause, there is no isekai/reincarnation/time-loop, the setting is a rural goshi estate with kamikakushi as believed folklore covering a human truth, and the late-game reset is a grounded season-cycle/founding-a-school time-skip that carries hard-won competence forward. No outright violations. The real work is hardening the deliberately-retained 'thin ambiguity' so its three stacked tokens (especially the guiding fox) can never be read as confirmed magic, locking returning-memory beats as flavor-only at the systems level so they never become a hidden stat, and treating the osso petition and the 'feral survivor' characters with historical and human care. Address the listed fixes and it is airtight.

candidate_spines.title_fit: "Kamikakushi" (spirited away) is the lie the village tells itself. When a barely-conscious stranger turns up half-dead at the edge of Akiyama lands with no memory and no travel papers, the god-fearing satoyama villagers prefer to say the mountain kami took him and gave him back. It is gentler, and safer, than the truth: that men beat him near to death on the highway and left him to die, and that those men live close by. The folk-belief is a community defense mechanism for not looking directly at the road and the violence on it. The whole game slowly corrects that comfortable supernatural story into a grim human one. Nobody was spirited away. Somebody was robbed, struck, and abandoned.
candidate_spines.premise_one_liner: A nameless, average 17-year-old farmhand wakes broken-headed on a lower-samurai estate with no past; the village calls it kamikakushi, but as he grinds himself from useless to merely capable, he uncovers that he stumbled onto a tax-skimming and smuggling racket on the highway, was beaten and left for dead, and that the people who did it are still moving rice and silver right past his new home.
candidate_spines.grounded_amnesia_cause: Blunt head trauma plus exposure. He was struck across the skull during a roadside robbery on the kaido (a club, the flat of a blade, a thrown rock), then lay in a ditch through a cold autumn rain for a day or two. The combination of concussion, blood loss, fever, and near-hypothermia produced genuine retrograde amnesia, a real and documented outcome of head injury. A traveling bone-setter (honetsugi) and the estate's old retainer treat him; his headaches, blank spots, and intrusive flashes of torchlight and shouting are concussion symptoms and stress-memory, never visions. No spirit, no curse, just a head wound that healed slowly and imperfectly, leaving him with a stranger's body and someone else's life to rebuild.
candidate_spines.central_mystery: Who am I, who hurt me, and why was I left to die? It is layered: (1) my own name and where I came from; (2) what I saw on the road that made me worth silencing; (3) why the local power structure wants the whole question buried under the kamikakushi story. The pull is that the answer is physically nearby and getting nearer. The same boundary-stones, packhorse trains, and night-river crossings that nearly killed him still run through the valley, so the mystery is not a distant quest but an ambient threat that intensifies the more competent and curious he becomes.
candidate_spines.grounded_resolution: He was Tahei, a farmhand and porter from a village two valleys north (Owada) that was crushed in a tax-revolt crackdown after a failed appeal (osso) over a rigged rice measure. Fleeing the reprisals as a porter on the Nakahara kaido, he was hired to carry a load he didn't understand: short-weighted tax rice being quietly resold and smuggled silver, run by the district's deputy magistrate (daikan's agent) Kuroiwa in partnership with the wholesale merchant Zeniya and protected by a masterless-swordsman enforcer, Hanzaki. Tahei saw the swap at the river ford, was recognized as a witness, and was beaten and dumped to look like a bandit victim. The "kamikakushi" rumor suited everyone: the village avoided admitting bandits worked their road, and Kuroiwa's people were glad to have a witness declared a ghost. The resolution is exposing the racket through evidence and testimony (the doctored measuring-box, the cargo manifests, a surviving fellow porter), not through a duel or magic. Tahei never regains a heroic past, because there isn't one; he was always ordinary, and the life worth having is the one he built at Akiyama.
candidate_spines.folklore_role: Folklore is the valley's emotional weather, never its physics. Kamikakushi is the headline rumor. Beyond it: villagers leave offerings at the dosojin boundary-stone where he was found, blame a tanuki for missing rice that is actually being skimmed, whisper of a kappa at the ford where bodies have surfaced, fear a "one-eyed mountain god" on the high pass, and hold a yamabushi ritual to "call back" spirited-away souls. Each belief is real to the people who hold it and each has a mundane cause underneath: the tanuki is theft, the kappa-ford is where smugglers sink evidence and the drowned, the one-eyed god is a scarred lookout for the smuggling route, the spirited-away are people who fled debt, conscription, or violence and were too ashamed or too dead to come back. The game treats these beliefs with respect, as how frightened people explain a dangerous world, while the player steadily learns the human truth beneath each.
candidate_spines.mediocre_start_honored: From the first minute he is plainly ordinary. He cannot remember a trade, but his hands know only farm and porter work; when he tries anything else he is clumsy. His starting stats are low and his early labor is slow and tiring (small rice yields, blisters, a back that aches). NPCs say so out loud: the old retainer notes his calluses and stoop mark him as a born field-hand, not a fighter; the estate's guard laughs that he holds a pole like a hoe. There is no hidden bloodline reveal, no moment where his body "remembers" combat training. His one genuine trait is stubbornness: he gets up again. The narrative explicitly frames his only assets as a strong back, a patient temper, and a refusal to quit, the traits of a good laborer, which is exactly what the incremental grind rewards.
candidate_spines.growth_through_effort: Every gain is visibly earned by repetition and time, never granted. Farming, foraging, woodcutting, and fishing improve through thousands of cycles (numbers go up because he keeps doing it). Combat readiness comes from a deliberate, humbling training loop: after his first near-fatal fight he begs the estate's aging guardsman Genza for drills, and his "strength" stat rises only through striking the makiwara post, hauling water, and sparring he keeps losing. The story names this directly: talent is a story the lucky tell; he has only the hours he puts in. Plateaus and soreness are diegetic (overtraining injuries force rest), reinforcing that growth is grind, not gift. By endgame he is genuinely capable, but the game is careful to show it took a year of dawns, so the player feels they built the character rather than discovered a chosen one.
candidate_spines.combat_framing: Early enemies are mundane and grounded: rats and boars raiding the rice, a fox in the henhouse, leeches and wasps in the satoyama, then human threats (a starving runaway, petty road-thieves, a debt-enforcer's muscle), and on the mountain, real dangers dressed as yokai by rumor (a rabid macaque troop, a wild boar said to be a curse-beast, a knife-wielding lookout called the one-eyed god). The humbling first fight: cornered defending the estate's grain store from two thieves, he swings a carrying-pole, lands one lucky blow, and is then thrashed, ribs cracked, only saved when Genza arrives. The game makes the lesson explicit, that he survived by luck and another man's blade, not skill, which is precisely what drives him to the training loop. No fight before that point should suggest competence beyond a scared farm boy flailing.
candidate_spines.yokai_handling: Each "yokai" is introduced as belief and resolved as cause, with a deliberately thin sliver of ambiguity left for unease rather than proof. The tanuki stealing rice resolves to a corrupt foreman skimming stores. The kappa at the river ford resolves to a smugglers' sinking-spot and the drownings of inconvenient people; the player physically recovers a weighted bundle from the pool. The one-eyed mountain god resolves to Hanzaki's scarred lookout posted to watch the pass. The spirited-away (including the protagonist) resolve to victims of violence, debt-flight, and crackdown. The yamabushi's "soul-calling" rite resolves to grief ritual that nonetheless gives a real clue (the priest kept a register of every vanished traveler). Ambiguity kept light: one flash of memory matches no known fact; a single offering at the boundary-stone is replaced by a hand the player never identifies. Enough to keep the night woods uneasy, never enough to confirm the supernatural.

act_structure.act: Act 0 — Waking (Prologue / onboarding)
act_structure.summary: He wakes on a pallet in an outbuilding of the Akiyama goshi estate, bandaged, nameless, terrified. The village has already decided he was kamikakushi-taken and returned. The estate's heir, Akiyama Soemon, lets him stay if he works; the old retainer Tsuru tends his head wound. Onboarding is minimal UI: one task, harvest rice, to eat. This act teaches that he is a poor worker who must repeat to survive, and seeds the first intrusive flash (torchlight, a river, a shout) that he cannot explain.

act_structure.act: Act 1 — Earning a place (the labor economy unlocks)
act_structure.summary: Through seasons of grind he becomes a trusted hand: farming, then foraging, woodcutting, and fishing unlock one at a time as he proves reliable and as NPCs grant access to the fields, woodlot, and river. The rice/koku economy comes online. Folklore texture thickens: missing rice blamed on a tanuki, the feared ford, offerings at the stone where he was found. He grows attached to the estate, especially to Tsuru and the miller's daughter Ono. The act ends with the humbling grain-store fight that nearly kills him.

act_structure.act: Act 2 — Learning to stand (training and the first threads)
act_structure.summary: Recovering, he begs Genza for training; the combat/training systems unlock as a grind loop he keeps failing. Idle auto-combat against vermin and the mountain's lesser dangers opens. Investigating the thieves and the 'tanuki' leads to the skimming foreman and the first hard fact: the road through the valley moves more rice than the books admit. A recovered manifest scrap and a fellow porter's name, Rokusuke, hint that the protagonist was on that road. The deputy Kuroiwa takes a too-keen interest in the 'spirited-away boy.'

act_structure.act: Act 3 — The road remembers (the conspiracy and the mountains)
act_structure.summary: Now capable, he pushes into the dangerous mountains and the ford, unlocking the deep gathering/combat tiers. He debunks the kappa (smugglers' sinking-pool, weighted evidence, a corpse) and the one-eyed god (Hanzaki's lookout). The racket comes clear: Kuroiwa and the merchant Zeniya short-weight tax rice and smuggle silver; Hanzaki enforces it. A flashback fact lands, that he was the porter Tahei who saw the swap. Now a known witness again, he and the estate are threatened; an attempt is made to silence him and frame the Akiyama for the missing tax.

act_structure.act: Act 4 — Reckoning (climax)
act_structure.summary: Not a magic duel but a case. He gathers evidence (the doctored masu measuring-box, manifests, Rokusuke's testimony, the yamabushi's register) to expose the racket to a higher authority (the touring inspector / metsuke) before Kuroiwa can bury it and the Akiyama with it. A grounded, hard-won confrontation: Genza's last lessons, the estate standing together, a brutal fight with Hanzaki that he wins by endurance and everything he trained, not talent. Kuroiwa falls; Zeniya's house is broken. He learns his real name and the death of his home village, mourning a past he cannot return to.

act_structure.act: Act 5 — The new dawn (diegetic reset / new game-plus)
act_structure.summary: The estate, vindicated but strained, and the valley needs its road made safe and its young taught to defend it. With Genza too old, Soemon asks Tahei to found a small training hall (a goshi-run dojo / mutual-aid watch) for the valley's farm boys. Time-skips a season; he sets out as a teacher carrying forward his hard-won skill and reputation (meta-progress) into a fresh cycle of building, now starting others from zero exactly as he started, the theme made mechanical.

candidate_spines.diegetic_reset: Late game, after the racket is broken, the valley is safer but unguarded and the Akiyama owe a debt to the boy who saved their name. Soemon grants Tahei a patch of land and his blessing to found a small training hall and mutual-defense watch for the valley's farmhands, since aging Genza can no longer drill them and the road must never again be lawless. A one-season time-skip frames the reset. Tahei's earned skills, koku, tools, and reputation carry forward as meta-progress (now as a master/employer rather than a hand), while the moment-to-moment grind restarts at the bottom of a new ladder: he is now teaching others from zero, recruiting and training raw farm boys exactly as ordinary as he once was. The fantasy of effort over talent becomes literally generational and mechanical, no reincarnation, no loop, just a man who climbed once now building the ladder for others.

key_npcs.name: Akiyama Soemon
key_npcs.role: Young heir of the lower-samurai (goshi) estate, mid-20s, dutiful but cash-poor and overshadowed by the district deputy
key_npcs.function: Quest-giver and gatekeeper of unlocks; grants the protagonist the right to stay and work, then progressively grants access to fields, woodlot, river, and finally land of his own. His honor and the estate's threatened reputation are the emotional stakes of the climax.

key_npcs.name: Tsuru
key_npcs.role: Old retainer and de facto nurse of the estate, sharp-tongued, has seen the valley's secrets for fifty years
key_npcs.function: Mentor and lore-keeper; treats his head wound (grounding the amnesia), names his symptoms, gently debunks superstition, and doles out the valley's history. The warmest relationship; her quiet faith that he is 'just a good boy who was hurt' anchors the human core.

key_npcs.name: Genza
key_npcs.role: The estate's aging lone guardsman, a competent but never-great old soldier with bad knees
key_npcs.function: Combat mentor and the embodiment of the no-talent theme. Saves the protagonist in the humbling first fight, then runs the brutal training loop. Explicitly teaches that he himself was never gifted, only persistent. His decline motivates the founding of the training hall.

key_npcs.name: Ono
key_npcs.role: The miller's daughter, practical and funny, runs the village's informal news
key_npcs.function: Peer relationship and information broker; the human reason to stay and protect the valley, and a conduit for rumor-as-gameplay (which yokai gossip points to which real crime). Her father's mill ties into the rigged rice-measure clue.

key_npcs.name: Kuroiwa
key_npcs.role: The district deputy magistrate's agent (tedai/daikan's man), outwardly lawful, the architect of the tax-skimming and smuggling racket
key_npcs.function: Primary antagonist; the human power that wants the kamikakushi story to stick. Polite, patient, and dangerous; tightens the noose as the protagonist grows curious. Defeated by evidence and testimony, not violence.

key_npcs.name: Zeniya Kihei
key_npcs.role: Wholesale rice-and-pawn merchant who launders the short-weighted tax rice and finances the smuggled silver
key_npcs.function: Secondary antagonist and economic engine of the conspiracy; ties the crime to the koku economy the player grinds, making the numbers-go-up theme thematically loaded. His ledgers are key climax evidence.

key_npcs.name: Hanzaki
key_npcs.role: Masterless swordsman (ronin) enforcer, scarred over one eye, the man behind the 'one-eyed mountain god' rumor and likely the one who struck the protagonist down
key_npcs.function: The physical threat and the mirror: a man of real talent gone rotten, contrasted with the protagonist's earned, ordinary competence. The climactic fight is won by trained endurance, not by out-talenting him.

key_npcs.name: Rokusuke
key_npcs.role: A surviving fellow porter from the night of the robbery, now hiding and crippled with fear
key_npcs.function: Living witness and memory-key; finding and protecting him both unlocks the protagonist's true identity (Tahei) and supplies the testimony that brings the racket down.

key_npcs.name: Genkai
key_npcs.role: A wandering yamabushi mountain-ascetic who performs the 'soul-calling' rite for the spirited-away
key_npcs.function: Folklore voice and unexpected clue-source; his ritual is grief-work, but the register he keeps of every vanished traveler is hard evidence that ties the disappearances to the road and the racket.

candidate_spines.emotional_hook: The ache of being no one, and the dignity of becoming someone anyway. The player begins with nothing, no name, no past, no skill, no place, and the game's deepest pleasure is the slow accrual of belonging: a bed that is his, a field the estate trusts him with, people who would notice if he died. When he finally learns his real name and that his home and family were destroyed, the gut-punch is that there is nothing to go back to, and the redemptive turn is that he doesn't need to, because he built a life worth more than the one that was stolen. It honors the player's literal investment: the hours of grind are the character's hard-won belonging, so the numbers going up feel like a person growing roots.
candidate_spines.how_it_paces_unlocks: Each act gates a tier of systems behind earned trust and earned skill, so the UI's incremental reveal is diegetically motivated. Act 0 shows only one task (harvest to eat) because he is a barely-conscious invalid. Act 1 unlocks farming then foraging, woodcutting, fishing one at a time as NPCs literally grant access to each resource (the woodlot key, permission to fish the estate stretch), bringing the rice/koku economy online. The humbling fight gates combat: only after nearly dying does training and idle auto-combat unlock, and only against vermin at first. Investigation gates exploration: each debunked folk-belief opens a new, more dangerous area (ford, then mountains, then the pass), which in turn unlocks deeper gathering and tougher idle combat. The conspiracy gates crafting and economy depth (forging tools, trading koku) because solving the case requires resources and allies. The final reset reframes every system around teaching, restarting the ladder while keeping meta-progress, so the incremental loop never runs dry.
risks: Mystery payoff may underwhelm if 'corrupt official runs a smuggling racket' feels generic; needs specific, surprising human texture (the rigged masu measure, the village's complicity in the kamikakushi lie) to land as more than a stock crime plot.

Grinding incremental systems can drown the narrative; story beats must be tied to concrete thresholds (koku totals, skill tiers, area unlocks) so players actually reach them, and the pacing risks feeling slow if a beat is gated behind too much grind.

The no-magic rule plus a folklore setting risks the 'it was a man in a mask all along' anticlimax (Scooby-Doo problem); the thin ambiguity must be handled with restraint so debunking feels like dawning dread, not a letdown.
Tonal whiplash between cozy Edo labor and grim violence/crime; needs deliberate modulation so the peaceful farm texture and the brutal road-violence reinforce rather than undercut each other.

Honoring the mediocre-start arc fights the genre's dopamine of fast power growth; if early grind is too punishing players quit before the payoff, so the humbling must read as motivating, not demoralizing.

The diegetic reset (founding a dojo, teaching from zero) could feel like a grind-reset punishment rather than a reward if meta-progress and the thematic 'building the ladder for others' framing aren't made tangible and satisfying.
Amnesia-protagonist and corrupt-official tropes are well-worn; the freshness must come from execution, specificity, and the genuinely grounded resolution, not from the premise itself.

Edo-period authenticity (goshi status, daikan/tedai administration, osso appeals, kaido porter culture, masu measures) must be researched enough to feel real without becoming a history lecture that stalls gameplay.

candidate_spines.title_fit: "Kamikakushi" (spirited-away) is the only story the Akiyama village can tell to explain a strange unconscious teenager appearing at the edge of their mountain estate during the Bon season — when, by custom, the dead and the spirits are closest. Calling it kamikakushi lets the village explain the inexplicable, perform the soothing rituals (lanterns, the Jizo offerings, the appeasement of the mountain kami) and, crucially, avoid asking the harder, more shameful human questions: who lets a child wander half-dead off a logging trail, and why did no one come looking? The grounded truth is that "spirited away" is what a community says instead of "we failed to notice." The folk-belief is the village's comfort and its cover; the game peels it back to reveal that every "spiriting" in the valley — the protagonist's, and the older one buried in the estate's past — has a mundane, human author.
candidate_spines.premise_one_liner: A nobody 17-year-old wakes with no memory on a struggling lower-samurai estate that takes them in as a "spirited-away" stray; by working the land season after season and slowly earning the household's trust, they uncover that the estate's whispered curse is really a decade-old human crime the family buried — and that their own forgotten past is far smaller and sadder than the legend everyone wants to believe.
candidate_spines.grounded_amnesia_cause: A concussion plus exposure, nothing more. The protagonist slipped on a rain-loosened logging trail on Mt. Kageyama after dark, struck their head on rock, and lay feverish and soaked through one cold mountain night before a charcoal-burner found them. The retrograde amnesia is the ordinary, medically real kind: the hours around the head injury are simply gone, and the fever blurred the days before. Memory returns not in a flash but in mundane fragments triggered by labor and smell — the feel of a sickle, the smell of a particular paddy mud — exactly the way real concussed memory creeps back. No spirit took the memories; a rock did.
candidate_spines.central_mystery: Two nested questions the player is driven to answer. The surface question (which the village answers with superstition): "Who am I and how did I get here?" The deeper question that hooks the player once they're embedded: "What is wrong with this house?" — why the Akiyama estate is quietly broke despite good land, why the family never speaks of the eldest son Akiyama Soutarou who "left for Edo" ten years ago, why the locked storehouse is off-limits, why the steward Kuroda is so feared, and why the villagers cross to the far path rather than pass the estate's north field at dusk. The player chases the household's secret because they, the memoryless outsider with no loyalties, are the one person who can ask.
candidate_spines.grounded_resolution: The estate's "curse" is debt, a cover-up, and a death. Ten years ago the head of house, Akiyama Tadeie, secretly mortgaged the estate's koku to an Osaka rice-broker to cover a failed silk-thread venture; to keep up appearances he squeezed the tenant farmers brutally. His eldest son and heir, Soutarou, discovered the fraud and confronted both his father and the steward Kuroda Genpachi — and during that confrontation Soutarou died: not murdered in cold blood, but killed in a violent shove during a struggle on the storehouse stairs (Kuroda's hand, Tadeie's complicity in the lie). They told the village he'd "gone to Edo," then later that he too had been "spirited away," and buried him under the north field, which is why no one walks there at dusk and why the family's grief reads as a haunting. The protagonist's own past is deliberately small: they are a runaway indentured orphan from neighbouring Haneda village, fleeing a cruel master, who got lost on the mountain — no destiny, no connection to the Akiyamas at all. That mundaneness is the point: the legend the village built around the protagonist is a mirror of the legend they built around Soutarou, both lies told to avoid an ugly human truth.
candidate_spines.folklore_role: Folklore is the valley's emotional grammar — never literal, always load-bearing as atmosphere and as a lens on human guilt. The kamikakushi belief explains the protagonist; Bon lanterns and the Jizo at the trailhead are offered "so the mountain gives nothing else away." The "yokai" of the north field (a vengeful spirit said to moan at dusk) is the wind through Soutarou's unmarked grave-marker and the family's stifled mourning. A "river-child" (kappa) scare at the irrigation weir is a drowning hazard and a missing tool. "Fox-fire" on the ridge is the charcoal-burners' night kilns and, once, a lantern someone carried to a grave. The old women's tale that "the Akiyama line is cursed to lose its sons" is the literal social memory of Soutarou's death, mythologized. Every superstition is a true feeling wearing a false face, and the game lets the player feel the unease before it shows them the human cause.
candidate_spines.mediocre_start_honored: The protagonist is established as genuinely unremarkable through both text and mechanics. The opening labor is fumbled: the first rice-cutting is slow, the first axe-swing glances off and the woodcutter laughs; NPCs note the newcomer is "soft-handed, town-soft, no use yet." There is no hidden skill — the protagonist doesn't read or write at first, doesn't know one rice variety from another, and is openly described by the steward as "a mouth to feed." Crucially, the backstory rules out any secret edge: an indentured orphan from a poorer village than this one, with nothing but a stubborn refusal to be sent away. The fantasy on offer is explicitly "you start with nothing and below-average, and you stay average until the work makes you otherwise."
candidate_spines.growth_through_effort: Every number that goes up is sweat-equity, and the story keeps saying so. Stamina, strength, and skill rise only through repeated labor and deliberate practice — the incremental loop IS the training montage. The old retainer Jinbei, a once-decent spearman now half-lame, agrees to teach not because the protagonist shows promise but because they keep showing up at dawn after being knocked flat; his refrain is "you have no gift — good, gifts make men lazy." Reputation with the household is grindable trust, not charm. Combat competence is literally a function of hours: the auto-resolving fights improve as conditioning and drilled forms accumulate. The thematic spine — that perseverance beats both talent and the supernatural, that an ordinary person who refuses to quit can outwork a curse — is stated by Jinbei early and proven in the finale, where the protagonist's edge over the steward is not strength but the patience to keep digging where everyone else looked away.
candidate_spines.combat_framing: Early "enemies" are grounded countryside hazards, not monsters: a boar trampling the millet, crows and a thieving fox, a rabid tanuki, leeches and a viper in the paddies, then human threats — a pair of road-bandits shaking down travelers on the pass. The humbling first fight is the boar (or the first bandit): the protagonist, having only ever swung a hoe, barely survives — gored/beaten, saved by Jinbei or sheer luck, and laid up for days. The game makes it unmistakable that this near-death is normal for an untrained farmhand and that survival was not skill. That beating is the explicit trigger to begin Jinbei's drills; the player feels the gap between "I have a stick" and "I can use it," and the incremental combat numbers exist to close that gap honestly. There is no point where the protagonist fights "better than a farmhand should" — they fight exactly as well as the logged training hours allow.
candidate_spines.yokai_handling: Each yokai is introduced as fearful rumor, investigated through ordinary work, and resolved to a concrete cause — but the game lingers in the unease before resolving, and leaves one or two edges deliberately, faintly unexplained for atmosphere. The north-field moaning ghost resolves to wind, an unmarked grave, and the family's hidden mourning. The weir-kappa resolves to undertow and a child's lost geta. The mountain "tengu" that locals blame for lost travelers resolves to the genuinely deadly trail and weather that nearly killed the protagonist themselves. The ambiguous edges kept light and unconfirmed: who relit the grave-lantern the night before the player finds the truth (was it Tadeie's widow, or Jinbei, or no one?), and whether the protagonist's last fever-dream memory of "a voice telling them to keep walking" was a hallucination or a real charcoal-burner — never confirmed, never magic, just the human mind in the dark. No yokai is ever shown to be real; the resolution is always a person, a hazard, or grief.

act_structure.act: Act 1 — The Stray (Waking & Earning a Bowl)
act_structure.summary: The protagonist wakes in the charcoal-burner's hut, is brought to the Akiyama estate, and is grudgingly kept on as the village murmurs 'kamikakushi.' Texture: learn to farm — the core farm/forage/woodcut loop unlocks here, fumbled and slow. Aki (the kind daughter) befriends them; steward Kuroda treats them as a burden. The boar attack ends the act: a humbling near-death that pushes the protagonist to beg old Jinbei for training. Hook: small wrongnesses noticed but not understood (the locked storehouse, the avoided north field, hunger despite good land).

act_structure.act: Act 2 — Soft Hands, Hard Work (Belonging & Training)
act_structure.summary: Seasons turn. Through relentless drilling with Jinbei (combat/conditioning systems unlock) and steady labor (rice economy/koku, crafting, fishing unlock), the protagonist becomes useful and trusted. Defending the estate from bandits on the pass earns real standing. Earned trust opens doors literally and socially: Aki confides the family never speaks of her brother Soutarou; Jinbei lets slip a date that doesn't match the official story. The protagonist starts to feel that their own forgotten past matters less than the house's. Hook deepens: the 'curse' is clearly hiding something.

act_structure.act: Act 3 — What the House Buried (Investigation)
act_structure.summary: The protagonist, now indispensable and unburdened by loyalty, follows the threads: ledgers in the storehouse showing the Osaka debt and squeezed tenants; the discrepancy in Soutarou's 'departure'; the widow's grief at the north field; the fox-fire that was a grave-lantern. Investigation is paced by reputation and access earned through labor. Meanwhile the protagonist's own memory returns in mundane fragments, leading to neighbouring Haneda — and the small, sad truth that they are just a runaway orphan, no one special. The two mysteries converge: both 'spiritings' were lies to hide shame.

act_structure.act: Act 4 — The North Field (Reckoning)
act_structure.summary: The protagonist confronts the truth of Soutarou's death — Kuroda's violent hand, Tadeie's complicity and the debt that drove it. Climax is human, not supernatural: a tense confrontation where the protagonist's only weapon is the patiently gathered proof and the hard-won standing to be believed by the household and, ultimately, the village headman/magistrate. Kuroda faces justice; the broken Tadeie is exposed; Soutarou is finally given a real grave. Aki and the widow can grieve honestly. No magic ever appears; the 'curse' lifts because the lie is gone.

act_structure.act: Act 5 — A New Season (The Grounded Reset)
act_structure.summary: With the Akiyama household scarred but freed, the protagonist — now competent, respected, and finally remembering who they were — chooses a future.
