The diegetic reset: a one-year time-skip / setting out to restore the failing estate's lands (or found a small training-and-farming dojo for other strays), carrying forward all hard-won skill and reputation as meta-progress. New systems and a wider map of the valley unlock; the cycle of seasons begins again at a higher tier, the protagonist now the steady hand others lean on.

candidate_spines.diegetic_reset: A one-year time-skip framed as the protagonist setting out to rebuild. After the reckoning, the estate is impoverished but honest; the protagonist, now trusted and skilled, is asked (or chooses) to take over restoring the Akiyama lands and to take in the next 'strays' the mountain coughs up — effectively founding a small working-farm-and-training-house. The season-cycle resets to spring, the map widens to the whole valley, and ALL meta-progress carries forward grounded as muscle memory, reputation, tools, and unlocked knowledge — you are simply a year stronger and more respected, not reborn. It reframes the incremental 'prestige' as a veteran starting a bigger venture, with the satisfying loop of teaching others the very labor you fumbled in Act 1. No reincarnation, no loop, no magic — just the next, larger season of a life rebuilt by work.

key_npcs.name: Akiyama Aki
key_npcs.role: Younger daughter of the house, ~16
key_npcs.function: The guide and emotional heart — she's the one who insists the stray be kept on, teaches the protagonist the estate's rhythms, and is the first to trust them. Gameplay: tutorializes the farm/economy loop and gates early reputation. Narrative: her unspoken grief for her vanished brother Soutarou is the thread that pulls the protagonist into the house's secret; she's the person the truth will most hurt and most free.

key_npcs.name: Jinbei
key_npcs.role: Old retainer, half-lame former spearman
key_npcs.function: The mentor who embodies the no-talent/all-effort theme. Gameplay: unlocks and paces the combat/conditioning systems; trains the protagonist only after they prove they won't quit. Narrative: a keeper of the household's old timeline whose offhand contradictions of the official story are early clues; ultimately an ally who wants the truth out but lacks the standing to force it.

key_npcs.name: Kuroda Genpachi
key_npcs.role: Estate steward (karō/bantō figure)
key_npcs.function: The antagonist — feared enforcer who squeezes the tenants and guards the storehouse. Gameplay: the social obstacle (low trust, blocked access) the player must work around and eventually overcome. Narrative: the hand that killed Soutarou in the stairwell struggle and the architect of the cover-up; not a cartoon villain but a man who chose loyalty-to-the-lie and self-preservation over justice.

key_npcs.name: Akiyama Tadeie
key_npcs.role: Head of house, the goshi lord
key_npcs.function: The tragic, complicit figure. Gameplay: the distant authority whose approval is the protagonist's high-end reputation goal. Narrative: drove the disaster with his secret debt and pride, lost his heir to it, and has lived a decade poisoned by the lie — his exposure is sorrow, not triumph.

key_npcs.name: Akiyama Soutarou
key_npcs.role: The dead eldest son (absent/posthumous)
key_npcs.function: The buried mystery made human — the 'ghost' of the north field, the 'curse on the line.' He never appears alive; he is reconstructed through ledgers, testimony, and grief. His honest reburial is the act's resolution and the proof that the haunting was love and guilt, never a spirit.

key_npcs.name: Old Tomé
key_npcs.role: Charcoal-burner who found the protagonist
key_npcs.function: Quest-giver and folklore voice — first contact, mountain-lore source, supplier of the 'tengu took him' rumor. Gameplay: gates woodcutting/foraging/mountain content. Narrative: the living reminder that the protagonist's rescue was mundane luck, and a witness whose half-memory of fox-fire on the ridge is a key clue.

key_npcs.name: Headman Mori Kichizō
key_npcs.role: Village headman (nanushi)
key_npcs.function: The bridge to justice and the wider economy. Gameplay: unlocks trade/market and village-tier systems; his trust gates Act 4. Narrative: complicit in the comfortable silence (kamikakushi is easier than scandal) until the protagonist's hard-won standing and proof force him to act.

candidate_spines.emotional_hook: The player cares because the protagonist's deepest wish — to belong, to matter, to be more than a discarded nobody — is something they have to earn in plain sight, with their hands, in a house that doesn't owe them anything. The amnesia makes the protagonist a blank the player pours themselves into; the grind makes belonging feel deserved. And the human core is grief and guilt: a family that lost a son and lied about it has been quietly rotting for ten years, and an outsider with nothing to lose can give them the unbearable gift of the truth. The catharsis isn't power — it's a teenager who was nobody becoming the person who lets a grieving sister finally mourn her brother by name. The protagonist's own forgettable past lands as bittersweet relief: you are not special, and you were never going to be saved by destiny — only by your own refusal to stop working, and by the people who chose to keep you.
candidate_spines.how_it_paces_unlocks: Story access and UI unlocks are the same currency: trust and competence, both earned by labor. Act 1 exposes only the basic survival loop (farm/forage/woodcut) because that's all a 'mouth to feed' is allowed to touch — the locked storehouse and barred north field are literally greyed-out UI the player can see but not open. Training with Jinbei in Act 2 unlocks combat/conditioning panels precisely when the boar humbling motivates them; rising household reputation unlocks crafting, fishing, the market, and—diegetically—keys, rooms, and conversations. Investigation in Act 3 is gated by reputation thresholds: you can only read the ledgers once you're trusted with the storehouse, only hear the widow once you've earned grief's confidence. The mystery reveals (clue panels, the family-timeline map, the Haneda memory fragments) are dripped as labor accrues, so the numbers-going-up and the truth-coming-out advance in lockstep. The Act 5 time-skip then re-gates a whole new valley-tier of systems behind the veteran's standing, restarting the unlock cadence at a higher floor.
risks: Mystery-vs-grind pacing: if investigation is gated purely behind long grinds, the human story can stall and feel like a chore — need clue drips frequent enough to keep narrative momentum between labor milestones.

Tonal whiplash: the cozy farm-sim texture and a buried-homicide mystery must be braided carefully so the dark turn (a son killed, a body in the field) doesn't feel tacked on or, conversely, drown the peaceful daily loop the genre needs.

Keeping folklore from tipping into real magic: every ambiguous edge (the relit lantern, the fever-voice) must stay genuinely unconfirmed; one too-convenient 'spooky' beat and the no-magic constraint is broken in players' minds.

Honoring the mediocre start under incremental pressure: incremental games reward fast early dopamine, which tempts a too-quick power curve that would betray the 'stays average until the work earns it' fantasy — early numbers must feel earned and humble.
Antagonist nuance: Kuroda and Tadeie must read as flawed humans (loyalty, pride, fear) rather than mustache-twirlers, or the grounded-human-tragedy thesis collapses into melodrama.

The deflating-own-past beat: revealing the protagonist is 'just a runaway orphan' is thematically vital but risks feeling anticlimactic if not framed as the intended, earned subversion of the kamikakushi legend.
Reset clarity: the Act 5 time-skip must clearly read as grounded meta-progress (a veteran starting bigger), not as a soft reincarnation, or it muddies the no-magic promise it's meant to uphold.

candidate_spines.title_fit: "Kamikakushi" (神隠し, "hidden by the gods") is the Edo folk-belief that someone who vanishes was spirited away — used here as the village's COPING LENS, never as truth. When the protagonist is found half-drowned, mud-caked, and memoryless below the estate, the villagers of Sawabe say the mountain kami took him and gave him back; that explanation is gentler than the real one and absolves everyone of looking too hard. The grounded truth the title hides: he was not hidden by gods but by water and by men — washed downriver in the night a flash flood erased his home village of Kuzuhara, and "hidden" again by the silence of the officials who let it happen. The folk-word for an unexplained disappearance becomes, by the end, the exact word for a deliberately unexplained disaster. The title is the village's first lie and the game's central irony.
candidate_spines.premise_one_liner: A nameless 17-year-old farmhand wakes with no memory on a lower-samurai estate, "returned by the kami" according to the village — but the truth is a flood drowned his home and a head wound stole his past, and to learn who he was and whether anyone else survived he must out-work, out-last, and out-train the limits of an ordinary boy.
candidate_spines.grounded_amnesia_cause: A flash flood. On a rain-swollen night the embankment/dam (the Kuzuhara seki) above his home village failed; the boy was swept down the Naka River, struck his head on rocks/debris, half-drowned, and was pulled from a weir-snag below the Tashiro estate at dawn. The amnesia is ordinary post-traumatic head injury plus near-drowning hypoxia and shock — confirmed in-fiction by a healing scalp gash the village herbalist dresses, by days of fever, and by classic patchy retrograde memory (he keeps simple skills and language but has lost autobiographical memory). No magic, no spirits — just a knock on the head and cold water.
candidate_spines.central_mystery: Three nested human questions that pull the player forward: (1) WHO AM I — name, home, family, the life behind the calluses on hands that can't remember earning them; (2) DID ANYONE ELSE SURVIVE — was he the only one carried out of Kuzuhara, or are there others scattered, hidden, or buried; and above all (3) WAS IT NATURE OR MEN — the flood killed a whole village in a region that had quietly survived worse, and the question that curdles everything is whether the embankment simply failed or whether it was let to fail (deferred maintenance, diverted corvée labor, or a famine-year decision to break it to save downstream rice fields and tax revenue). The mystery is not "what supernatural force did this" but "which neighbors, officials, and choices did this, and will anyone ever be made to answer."
candidate_spines.grounded_resolution: The boy is Ren, a tenant farmhand from Kuzuhara, an upstream hamlet wiped out in a flood three years into a regional famine. The embankment did not "just fail." During the famine the district magistrate's office, pressed for its rice quota (koku), had quietly defunded the corvée upkeep on the upstream seki and diverted both labor and relief-rice; a local headman covered the shortfall in the ledgers. When monsoon rains came, the neglected, undermined embankment gave way in the night and Kuzuhara — the poorest, lowest, least-defended hamlet — drowned while the well-maintained downstream dykes protecting the tax fields and the magistrate's interests held. It was negligence shading into a chosen sacrifice of the expendable. The "arson cover-up" angle resolves grounded too: the relief-storehouse that should have fed Kuzuhara burned (to hide that it was already empty), and the official story blamed the kami/an unlucky village. Resolution is NOT vengeance-magic: Ren can never undo it, and cannot simply kill his way to justice (he's one trained-up farmhand, not a hero). Instead he survives, recovers fragments of who he was, finds the few other scattered survivors, gathers the mundane proof (the doctored ledger, a surviving corvée tally-stick, a witness), and forces the truth into the open through testimony, petition, and refusal to let Kuzuhara be forgotten — a quiet, human, partial justice. He chooses to live, to teach others to last, and to keep the drowned village's name spoken.
candidate_spines.folklore_role: Folklore is the village's psychology made weather. Sawabe is a frightened satoyama community that survived the famine years and does not want to look at why it survived when others didn't, so it reaches for stories: the boy was kamikakushi'd and returned; the upstream valley is now cursed/haunted (kept off-limits — convenient for anyone hiding what's there); a kappa drags the unwary into the weir where Ren was found; foxes (kitsune) take the shape of strangers, so distrust the amnesiac; the gods sent the flood as punishment for impiety (which neatly blames the dead victims, not the living officials). The player constantly hears these beliefs as rumor, ritual (offerings at the river, a yearly placation festival), and gossip — and constantly watches them dissolve into human causes when examined. Crucially, folklore is treated with respect, not mockery: it is how grieving, powerless people make unbearable facts bearable, and abandoning the comforting story for the true one is part of Ren's (and the village's) hard growth.
candidate_spines.mediocre_start_honored: Ren begins genuinely unremarkable and the game says so out loud and mechanically. He is a 17-year-old tenant farmhand — strong enough for a day's stoop labor and nothing more. His first weeks are pure peasant work because that's all he can do and all the estate will trust him with: hauling water, weeding paddies, splitting kindling, with low, slow numbers. NPCs explicitly read him as ordinary: the estate steward calls him "another mouth," the kitchen takes pity, a guard sneers at the idea of him holding a weapon. There is NO discovered talent, NO bloodline reveal, NO "his body remembers being a swordsman." When fragments of his past surface, they reveal an equally ordinary life — a poor farmer's son, not a hidden warrior. The one thing he turns out to have is not skill but a temperament forged by surviving: stubbornness, the refusal to stop. That, not gift, is the only seed — and it's a character trait, not a power.
candidate_spines.growth_through_effort: Every number that goes up is sweat. The incremental economy IS the growth arc: repetition of farm/forage/woodcut/fish/craft slowly raises stamina, strength, and skill, and only that grind unlocks the capacity to train, then to defend himself, then to range into danger. Combat ability rises the same diegetic way — drilling forms against a strawman (wara-ningyo), conditioning by carrying logs, sparring and losing and trying again — explicitly framed as "you are not talented; you simply did it ten thousand times." Mentors say it plainly: skill is the residue of work. Plateaus are part of it: progress stalls and the answer is never a magic item but more reps, better technique, more rest, better tools you crafted. The meta-progress that survives the late-game reset is exactly this hard-won competence and reputation — proof that perseverance compounds. The fantasy the player lives is "an ordinary person who refused to quit became, over a long time, genuinely capable," and the mechanics never cheat that by gifting power.
candidate_spines.combat_framing: Early "enemies" are grounded countryside threats that any rural laborer plausibly faces: a boar wrecking the millet plot, a snake in the woodpile, monkeys raiding stores, a rabid feral dog, then human dangers — a lone deserter-thief on the road, then organized bandits (ronin and famine-driven brigands) preying on weak villages, and the genuinely lethal mountains (cold, falls, starvation, hunger-maddened animals). The "yokai" encounters are these same things distorted by fear. THE HUMBLING FIRST FIGHT: when bandits hit Sawabe (or when Ren, against advice, ventures up the cursed valley), he tries to stand and is nearly killed — disarmed, beaten, saved only by an NPC or by running; the game pointedly denies him competence ("you swing like you're chasing crows from the rice; the blade is heavier than any hoe, and he is faster, and stronger, and trained, and you are not"). He survives barely, ashamed, injured — and THAT humiliation is the explicit motivation to train. Combat thereafter is idle/auto-resolving and only tilts in his favor after long real grinding; for a long time the correct, survivable choice is to avoid, flee, or fight in numbers, not to be a hero.
candidate_spines.yokai_handling: Each named "yokai" is introduced as belief and resolved as a human/natural fact, with a thin deliberate margin of ambiguity kept for unease (never confirmed magic). The kappa of the weir = the drowning-prone snag and undertow where Ren was found, plus the very real corpses the flood deposited there. The "ghost lights"/hitodama drifting in the dead upstream valley = lantern-light of survivors and scavengers (and later the magistrate's men) moving through forbidden ground at night. The "vengeful spirit" of Kuzuhara haunting the ruins = a half-feral surviving girl living in the wreckage. The "yamauba"/mountain-hag of the high woods = an exiled old woman, herself a famine outcast. The "fox-spirit stranger" suspicion that dogs Ren = ordinary xenophobia toward an amnesiac outsider. The "curse" on the valley = the practical fact that powerful people benefit from it staying feared and empty. The retained ambiguity is atmospheric only: a recurring dream/half-memory of a voice calling his name in the water that may be trauma or may be someone real (it turns out to be real — a survivor — not a spirit); the player is allowed a shiver, never a spell.

act_structure.act: Act 0 — The Weir (Prologue / tutorial)
act_structure.summary: Ren wakes feverish in a storehouse on the Tashiro goshi estate, pulled half-drowned from the river. He has no name, no past, a dressed head wound, and hands that know labor he can't remember learning. The village herbalist Obaa Sato tends him; the village murmurs 'kamikakushi.' GROUNDED HOOK established: a flood, a head injury, an amnesiac stranger. UNLOCKS: only the most basic labor panel (haul water, pull weeds) and a sparse UI — you are a sick boy earning your keep.

act_structure.act: Act I — Earning Your Rice
act_structure.summary: Recovery through work. Ren becomes a trusted estate laborer: farming, foraging, woodcutting, fishing, basic crafting — the core incremental economy opens panel by panel as his stamina and reputation rise. Daily texture, peaceful, numbers climbing slowly. Threads planted: recurring water-dream of a voice calling a name; flashes of paddies that aren't Sawabe's; the off-limits 'cursed' upstream valley everyone refuses to discuss; the famine still fresh in everyone's fear. MOTIVATION: to go looking for his past he first needs to be strong and trusted enough to leave the estate.

act_structure.act: Act II — The First Blood
act_structure.summary: The countryside turns dangerous: vermin, then a boar, then road-thieves, culminating in a bandit raid on Sawabe (famine brigands). Ren tries to fight and is humiliatingly, nearly fatally beaten — the core humbling beat. Shamed, he begs the estate's old guard Tashiro Genzo to teach him. UNLOCKS: the training/combat systems, conditioning, the strawman drill — all gated behind grind. He also recovers a hard fragment: the name in the dream is 'Kuzuhara,' and Kuzuhara is the drowned upstream village no one will name. The mystery sharpens from 'who am I' to 'what happened up the valley.'

act_structure.act: Act III — The Drowned Valley
act_structure.summary: Now capable enough to range, Ren pushes into the forbidden upstream ruins. The 'haunting' resolves into a feral survivor girl, Kiku, who recognizes him — they were both Kuzuhara children. Together they uncover the disaster's shape: the broken seki, the bones, the burned relief-storehouse. The supernatural rumors fall away one by one into human facts. The new, darker question lands: the embankment didn't just fail — it was neglected and the failure was covered. UNLOCKS: deeper exploration/mountain expeditions, expedition logistics, and an investigation thread (gathering evidence, witnesses, the tally-stick).

act_structure.act: Act IV — The Ledger
act_structure.summary: Investigation against power. Ren and Kiku, with allies, assemble mundane proof: the doctored corvée-and-relief ledger, a surviving tally-stick, a witness (a guilt-sick minor clerk). They learn the magistrate's office diverted famine relief and embankment-upkeep labor and let Kuzuhara drown to protect downstream tax fields, then blamed the kami and burned the empty storehouse. Pressure mounts: the powerful want the valley to stay 'cursed' and forgotten; Ren is hunted as a troublesome witness. He still cannot win by force — proven again in a losing skirmish — only by getting the truth to someone who outranks the guilty (a circuit inspector / the domain) via formal petition (osso).

act_structure.act: Act V — The Petition & The Reset
act_structure.summary: The grounded climax: not a duel but a dangerous overland journey and a formal appeal that risks everything (peasant petitions were punishable). Partial human justice — the headman/clerk are exposed, some restitution and a shrine to Kuzuhara's dead are won; the highest official escapes clean, because that is how the world is. Ren gets his answers: his name, that his family did not survive, that he is one of a handful left. THE DIEGETIC RESET (see field): a season-turn time-skip into the next chapter of a now-competent life, carrying forward all hard-won skill and reputation.

candidate_spines.diegetic_reset: A grounded season-cycle time-skip framed as Ren setting out on his own and founding a small school/work-house. After the petition resolves, the calendar turns: Ren — no longer a nameless invalid but a known, capable young man — formally leaves the Tashiro estate (with Genzo's blessing and Kiku) to re-found Kuzuhara as a tiny resettlement and open a humble dojo-cum-workhouse for other famine orphans and displaced laborers, teaching them the same survive-by-effort skills he ground out. Mechanically this is the meta-progress reset: the cramped estate-tenant UI and its early caps are 'left behind,' the map/season resets to a fresh start with expanded systems, but ALL hard-won competence (skill levels, techniques, reputation, recipes, the relationships) carries forward as your starting baseline and as bonuses you now teach to NPC students (who themselves become idle producers/fighters). It reads as a life beginning again on firmer ground — no reincarnation, no loop, no magic — just an ordinary survivor who became someone, starting the harder work of helping others last too. New-Game-Plus is literally 'a new season, your own land, your own students.'

key_npcs.name: Tashiro Genzo
key_npcs.role: Aging goshi estate guard / failed-warrior elder
key_npcs.function: Mentor + combat-systems gatekeeper. A lower-samurai who never saw real war and knows it; teaches Ren that competence is only repetition, not gift. Unlocks training/combat after the humbling first fight. Quietly carries famine-era guilt (his estate held while others drowned), giving him a personal stake in the truth.

key_npcs.name: Obaa Sato
key_npcs.role: Village herbalist / wise-woman
key_npcs.function: Guide + the grounded voice. Dresses Ren's head wound (anchors the mundane amnesia), narrates folk-belief sympathetically while privately knowing it's coping, dispenses early survival/foraging/crafting knowledge. The compassionate human face of the village's denial.

key_npcs.name: Kiku
key_npcs.role: Feral survivor girl living in the drowned valley ruins
key_npcs.function: Mystery-key + co-investigator + emotional anchor. The 'ghost' of the cursed valley resolved into a person; a fellow Kuzuhara child who knew Ren before. She holds memory he lost and the will for justice he must earn. Becomes co-founder of the resettlement at the reset.

key_npcs.name: Steward Mochizuki
key_npcs.role: Tashiro estate steward / ledger-keeper
key_npcs.function: Quest-giver early (assigns labor, paces economy unlocks) and ambiguous-ally/obstacle later — he knows the estate benefited and the ledgers were touched; whether he helps or protects himself is a key human tension.

key_npcs.name: Headman Yag-emon
key_npcs.role: District village headman (shoya)
key_npcs.function: Antagonist-of-record. The local man who doctored the corvée/relief ledgers and parroted the 'cursed village' story to the magistrate. Not a monster — a frightened middle-man who chose his own village over Kuzuhara. The humanly culpable face Ren can actually reach.

key_npcs.name: Clerk Naozane
key_npcs.role: Guilt-sick junior clerk in the magistrate's office
key_npcs.function: Mystery-key / witness. The crack in the cover-up: he transcribed the lies and can't live with it. Provides the documentary proof and testimony for the petition — the grounded 'key' that replaces any magic revelation.

key_npcs.name: Magistrate Inoue
key_npcs.role: District deputy magistrate (daikan)
key_npcs.function: True antagonist / untouchable power. Diverted relief and upkeep to make quota during famine and let the expendable hamlet drown. Largely escapes justice — the grounded, bitter truth about power that keeps the resolution honest and non-heroic.

candidate_spines.emotional_hook: The ache of not knowing whether you are mourning anyone — and of being told a comforting lie about it. Ren carries grief with no object: hands that remember a family they can't picture, a name in the water he can't place, and a whole village willing to wrap his loss in 'the kami took you and gave you back' so no one has to feel the real weight. The player's drive is the most human one — to find out who they lost and to refuse to let those people be erased twice (first by the flood, then by the lie). Layered on top is the dignity of the underdog: the quiet, stubborn pride of an ordinary, talentless boy who is told constantly that he is nothing special and proves, through nothing but not-stopping, that he can still matter. The catharsis isn't winning — it's being able, finally, to say the drowned village's name out loud and make others hear it."
candidate_spines.how_it_paces_unlocks: Each act gates the next tier of systems behind a story-justified threshold, so the incremental UI grows with Ren's life. Act 0: invalid — only one or two labor actions, deliberately bare UI (he's a sick boy). Act I: trust + recovered stamina open the full peaceful economy panel by panel (farm → forage → woodcut → fish → craft → koku/rice economy), each tied to earning the estate's confidence. Act II: the humbling defeat unlocks the entire training/combat/conditioning suite — explicitly because he resolves to never be that helpless again, and it's grind-gated so the player feels the climb. Act III: 'now strong and trusted enough to leave' unlocks ranged exploration, mountain expeditions, and logistics (provisions, party). Act IV: the investigation unlocks an evidence/relationship/influence layer (gathering proof, swaying witnesses, reputation as social currency). Act V's reset re-opens the map at a higher tier with the management/teaching layer (students as idle producers) — the final breadth of UI. The player never gets a panel before the story makes Ren capable of and motivated for it, so the UI's incremental reveal and the narrative's growth-through-effort are the same curve.
risks: Tone slippage into the supernatural: the kappa/ghost-lights/'curse' material must ALWAYS visibly resolve to human causes; if any yokai is left genuinely magical it breaks the no-magic constraint. Mitigation: enforce a one-to-one 'belief → grounded explanation' table for every folklore element.

Accidentally gifting the protagonist talent: writers may be tempted to let his 'body remember' fighting or to reveal a warrior past — forbidden. Mitigation: his recovered past must stay ordinary (poor farmer's son), and every combat gain must be visibly grind-earned.

The humbling first fight feeling like a cheap scripted loss rather than earned helplessness — needs to read as 'he is simply outmatched and untrained,' with clear before/after so training feels causal.

Dark subject matter (a drowned village, official negligence, dead family) risking misery without hope — needs the counterweight of warm daily Edo labor, found-family with Kiku/Genzo, and the dignified, forward-looking reset so it lands as bittersweet, not bleak.

Justice resolution feeling either too neat (a satisfying villain-kill betrays the grounded 'power escapes' truth and the no-hero rule) or too hopeless (total failure deflates the player). Mitigation: deliberate PARTIAL justice — the reachable culprits answer, the untouchable one mostly doesn't, but Kuzuhara is remembered.

Pacing mismatch: gating systems behind story beats could starve incremental players of numbers-go-up if acts run long. Mitigation: keep the peaceful economy generously deep within each act so grinding is always rewarding even before the next narrative unlock.
Petition (osso) and famine-era administration details must stay historically plausible (peasant appeals were real and punishable) — light research needed so the grounded climax doesn't ring false.

candidate_spines.title_fit: "Kamikakushi" (神隠し, "hidden by the kami / spirited away") is the regional folk-explanation for any person who vanishes without trace — and the whole valley already has a famous case: a child named Tama who disappeared from Asagiri-mura years ago and was "taken by the mountain god." When a half-dead, memoryless 17-year-old turns up at the goshi estate, the village instantly reads it as the kami GIVING someone back. The title is the lie the village tells itself. The grounded truth honors the word two ways: (1) the original "kamikakushi" was a child running from a beating and a debt, hidden by ordinary human ugliness, not spirits; (2) the protagonist's own "spiriting away" is amnesia from a real injury after a real disaster. Nothing was ever hidden by gods — only by shame, debt, and the dark of the mountains.
candidate_spines.premise_one_liner: A mediocre 17-year-old farmhand wakes injured and memoryless at a remote lower-samurai estate where everyone is convinced he is Tama, the local child "spirited away by the kami" a decade ago — and must rebuild himself from nothing through sheer daily toil while quietly uncovering the very human truth of both disappearances.
candidate_spines.grounded_amnesia_cause: Blunt head trauma plus exposure. The protagonist (we'll call him by the name the village forces on him, "Tama," until he chooses his own) was a day-laborer/porter caught on a mountain pass during a violent autumn flood/landslide that wiped out a section of road and a porters' caravan he was traveling with. He took a rock to the skull, half-drowned in the swollen river, and was washed/crawled downstream into Asagiri valley. Retrograde amnesia from concussion + fever from infected wounds and days of exposure. Memory returns not in a flash but in slow, ordinary fragments (a smell, a knot he ties without thinking, a road that feels familiar) — never magically, always triggered by mundane sensation, and never restoring "hidden combat skill," only a hard, sad ordinary past.
candidate_spines.central_mystery: "Am I Tama, the child this whole valley mourns — or just an unlucky stranger who happens to look like the boy in their memory?" The player is driven to find out WHO they actually are, which forces the second question: "If I'm not Tama, then what really happened to the real Tama?" The two mysteries are deliberately tangled so the village's certainty (you ARE the spirited-away child, returned by the kami) is the red herring that keeps the player — and the player-character — from seeing the mundane truth of either disappearance.
candidate_spines.grounded_resolution: The protagonist is NOT Tama. The resemblance is real but coincidental-plus-suggestible: he's roughly the right age, has a similar build and a birthmark/scar near the same place, and — crucially — the people who "recognize" him are remembering a child they last saw at age seven through a decade of grief and guilt; memory and hope did the rest. The protagonist's true identity: an orphaned porter named Rokusuke from two valleys over, indentured to a transport guild to work off his dead father's debt; the caravan he died-but-didn't on the pass was a smuggling-tinged cargo run, which is why no one came looking (the guild wrote him off; admitting the route meant admitting the contraband). The REAL Tama's fate, uncovered piece by piece: Tama did not vanish into the mountains at all. Tama (a girl, not the boy the village "remembers" — the gender drift is itself a clue to how unreliable the legend is) ran away from a violent stepfather and a household that had quietly agreed to sell her into service to clear a rice debt to the estate; a kindly midwife and a traveling medicine-peddler helped her flee down-valley, and she has been alive and grown the whole time, working in a post-town, terrified to come back. The "kami took her" story was allowed to stand — even encouraged — by the adults who were complicit, because a comforting supernatural tragedy is easier to live with than the truth that they were about to sell a child. The protagonist's arc resolves by reuniting the village with the real, living Tama and freeing himself to own his real name and the new self he built by hand.
candidate_spines.folklore_role: Folklore is the valley's emotional weather, never its physics. The kamikakushi belief is load-bearing socially: it explains the unexplainable, absolves the guilty, and gives grief a shape. Concretely it shows up as — a roadside jizo and a "spirit gate" shimenawa where Tama was last seen; an annual obon-adjacent rite where the village leaves rice and a child's straw sandal at the mountain shrine "in case the kami brings them home"; the priest's and old women's readings of every odd event (a fox bark at dusk, ball-lightning over the ridge, missing chickens) as yokai activity; warnings about the yamanba (mountain hag), the tengu of the high cedars, kappa in the irrigation pond, and "the night the road walks." Each superstition is seeded as atmosphere and then quietly given a grounded cause the attentive player can assemble: the "fox-fire" is a charcoal-burner's hidden kiln; the "kappa" drownings are an undercut riverbank and a poacher's weighted nets; the "tengu" who steals tools is a deserter/fugitive ronin living rough in the high forest (and later a guarded source of mountain-survival knowledge). Ambiguity is preserved at the edges — one or two omens are never fully explained, so the unease never dies — but no event is ever CONFIRMED as magic, and the player never gains a power from any of it.
candidate_spines.mediocre_start_honored: The opening explicitly establishes weakness. The protagonist can barely stand for the first days (concussion, fever) — the earliest "gameplay" is literally resting, drinking water, and being spoon-fed millet gruel by the estate's people. His hands blister on the first hoe; he's slower than the ten-year-old who shows him the rice paddy; the head man's wife notes flatly that he's "soft, clumsy, and eats more than he earns." Dialogue repeatedly undercuts any heroism: when he ties a load-knot correctly on instinct, the carter says "huh, you've hauled before" — a porter's mundane habit, explicitly NOT a warrior's skill. There is no hidden bloodline, no sword that answers to him, no instinctive fighting. The text is careful: any returning memory is of ordinary hard labor and ordinary fear, never of competence he didn't earn. The village's belief that he's the "returned" Tama is the ONLY thing special about him, and it's false — a projection, not a power.
candidate_spines.growth_through_effort: Every number that goes up is sweat the player chose to spend. The incremental loop IS the theme: rice/koku, stamina, calloused-hands stat, and skill ranks (Hoeing, Foraging, Woodcutting, Fishing, Carrying, later Spear/Staff) all rise through repetition and perseverance, with diminishing returns that you push past only by showing up daily and never quitting — the literal zero-to-competent arc. The mentor's creed, stated early and paid off throughout: "Talent is a story the lucky tell. You are not lucky. So you will work." Combat skill is gated behind farm/labor stamina: you cannot fight bandits until months of carrying rice bales have given you a back and wind; the spear is "just a long hoe held with intent." Story beats reward grind with plot: the village only starts trusting/confiding in you as your reputation (a tracked stat earned by completed labor and harvests survived) climbs, which is what eventually unlocks the testimonies that crack the mystery. Perseverance is mechanized — there's a "kept at it" thread where failing/repeating a task enough times yields a breakthrough, dramatizing never-give-up rather than innate talent.
candidate_spines.combat_framing: Early enemies are strictly grounded mountain dangers: a cornered boar in the millet, monkeys raiding stores, a starving stray dog pack, leeches/hornets as environmental hazards, then human threats — a lone desperate rice-thief, then organized bandits (rōnin gone to banditry, the real menace of a poor Edo countryside). The "yokai" the village fears are always grounded foes wearing folklore's costume: the "kappa" of the pond is a poacher; the "yamanba" raiding huts is a starving displaced widow; the "tengu" is the fugitive in the cedars. THE HUMBLING FIRST FIGHT: weeks in, the protagonist is jumped on the wood-gathering trail by a single bandit. He loses, badly — disarmed, beaten, robbed of the estate's hatchet, and left in the dirt; only luck (a passing carter, or the bandit's contempt) spares his life. He limps home humiliated, having to confess he lost the estate's tool. This defeat is the engine: it shames him into begging the old retainer to teach him, and it makes the player FEEL why the slow training grind matters. Combat stays idle/auto-resolving thereafter, but its outcomes are explicitly a function of the stamina and skill you ground out through peaceful labor — you win later fights because you trained, not because you were ever gifted.
candidate_spines.yokai_handling: Each named yokai is introduced as genuine village dread, investigated as a quest line, and resolved as a human/natural cause — but the resolution is delivered with restraint so the world keeps its hush. The kappa = a drowned-cattle scare caused by an undercut bank and a poacher using weighted nets (you find the nets, not a beast). The yamanba = Oyoné, a widow burned out of a neighboring hamlet by the same flood, surviving feral on the ridge; "defeating" her is really feeding and resettling her — a mercy quest, not a kill. The tengu of the high cedars = a fugitive ex-foot-soldier, Genza, who becomes a wary teacher of mountain craft and tracking (and a key witness, since he saw who really used the pass). The "spirited-away road" / night the road walks = landslide-prone terrain and disorienting fog where travelers genuinely die — exactly how the protagonist arrived. Deliberate residual ambiguity: a single fox that seems to lead the protagonist to safety more than once, and one omen at the shrine that lines up too neatly, are never explained — the game lets the player and the priest each keep their own reading. No yokai is ever shown to be real; none of them grant or explain any power.

act_structure.act: Act I — The Returned (Tutorial / Waking)
act_structure.summary: Protagonist wakes injured and nameless at the Kurosawa goshi estate; the steward's daughter Sayo names him 'Tama' on sight, and word races through Asagiri-mura that the kami have returned the spirited-away child. Gameplay is minimal and incremental: rest, eat, then the first humble labors (hauling water, hoeing, foraging) under the old retainer Jūbei. The whole UI is just a body-bar, a rice counter, and one or two tasks. Hook: the village treats you as a miracle while you can barely lift a hoe. Ends when you survive your first harvest task and earn a sleeping-place — you've chosen to stay and find out who you are.

act_structure.act: Act II — Calluses (The Grind & the Humbling)
act_structure.summary: Daily labor economy opens up (farm, forage, woodcut, fish, craft → rice/koku); reputation begins to climb and unlock village memory of Tama. The humbling first bandit defeat on the wood trail forces you to beg Jūbei for training; the combat/training panels unlock, gated behind labor-built stamina. You meet the grounded 'yokai' as fearsome rumors (kappa, yamanba, tengu). Drives the player to grind because both survival and trust visibly require it. Ends with you strong enough to walk the mountain trails without dying — and noticing the Tama story doesn't add up (people 'remember' different ages, even a different gender).

act_structure.act: Act III — The Things People Remember (Investigation)
act_structure.summary: Mid-game systems fully bloom: mountain expeditions, crafting tiers, bandit-clearing, the seasonal/koku economy at scale. As reputation buys confessions, the kamikakushi legend unravels — Genza the 'tengu' was a witness, Oyoné the 'yamanba' knew the family, the midwife and priest hold guilty silences. You learn the real Tama may have run, not vanished, and that adults colluded in the spirit-story to bury a near-sale of a child for rice debt. Simultaneously your own fragments return (a porter's knots, a debt-token, the smuggling route) hinting you are not Tama at all. Ends on the gut-punch: you find proof Tama was a girl who fled — meaning you were never her.

act_structure.act: Act IV — Your Own Name (Truth & Choice)
act_structure.summary: You track the threads down-valley: the medicine-peddler, the post-town, and finally the living, grown Tama (now 'Oharu') who fears the village that almost sold her. You also confirm your own identity as Rokusuke, the indentured porter the guild wrote off. Climax is human, not martial: confronting the village (and the estate's complicity in the rice debt) with the truth, and brokering Tama's safe, voluntary return or peace. The valley's comforting myth dies; something honest replaces it. You can keep the name Tama gave you a home under, or claim Rokusuke — either way it's now earned, not assigned.

act_structure.act: Act V — Spring Again (Diegetic Reset / New Cycle)
act_structure.summary: A grounded time-skip: a full season turns. Hardened by a year of labor and the truth you forced into the light, you're offered (and accept) standing of your own — Jūbei, aging, makes you the estate's new field-and-trail man, or you set out to found a small training/portering outfit. The world resets to a fresh season-cycle and a larger map, but your hard-won skill, reputation, and tools carry forward as meta-progress. New rumors of kamikakushi drift up from farther valleys — fresh mysteries, same grounded promise. The cycle of work-and-truth begins again at a higher tier.

candidate_spines.diegetic_reset: A grounded SEASON-CYCLE time-skip, not reincarnation or a loop. After the truth is exposed, a full year/season turns and the protagonist is formally given a role of his own — aging Jūbei hands him stewardship of the estate's fields and mountain trails (or he sets out to found his own small portering-and-training outfit with Genza and Oharu). Mechanically this is the meta-progress carry-over: your earned skill ranks, your reputation, your best tools and stockpiled koku persist, the map and economy expand to a higher tier, and the daily loop resumes fresh at spring planting. It's framed as the natural Edo rhythm of seasons and of an apprentice becoming a master — hard-won competence compounding, never a magical do-over. New, farther-off kamikakushi rumors seed the next cycle's mysteries.

key_npcs.name: Jūbei
key_npcs.role: Aging lower-samurai retainer of the Kurosawa estate; old foot-soldier turned overseer
key_npcs.function: Mentor & combat/training gatekeeper. Speaks the 'talent is a story the lucky tell — so you will work' creed; refuses to train you until you've earned it through labor and your humbling defeat. Embodies the growth-through-effort theme; later a moral conscience as estate complicity surfaces.

key_npcs.name: Sayo
key_npcs.role: Steward's daughter, ~16, sharp and kind
key_npcs.function: Guide & emotional anchor. She's the one who names you 'Tama' and wants most desperately for you to be him (Tama was her childhood friend) — so her hope is the heart of the red herring. Quest-giver for village life; her grief and eventual reckoning with the truth is the emotional spine.

key_npcs.name: Genza (the 'tengu')
key_npcs.role: Fugitive ex-foot-soldier / deserter living rough in the high cedars
key_npcs.function: Grounded-yokai reveal, mountain-craft teacher, and key witness. Feared as a tengu; becomes a wary mentor in tracking/survival and a holder of evidence about who really used the pass — bridging both mysteries.

key_npcs.name: Oyoné (the 'yamanba')
key_npcs.role: Widow burned out by the flood, surviving feral on the ridge
key_npcs.function: Grounded-yokai reveal + mercy quest. 'Defeating' her means feeding and resettling her; she once knew Tama's household and supplies a crucial confession about the debt and the family's violence.

key_npcs.name: Obaba Toki, the midwife
key_npcs.role: Old village midwife who attended Tama's birth and helped her flee
key_npcs.function: Mystery-key & guilty conscience. Holds the truth that Tama ran (and lives), kept silent for a decade to protect the girl. Her late-game confession, earned through reputation, cracks the case open.

key_npcs.name: Priest Ekai
key_npcs.role: Keeper of the mountain shrine and the kamikakushi rites
key_npcs.function: Folklore-voice & ambiguity-keeper. Frames every event in supernatural terms and benefits from the comforting myth; an antagonist-of-belief (not of malice) whose worldview the truth must dismantle — yet who keeps one unexplained omen to himself at the end.

key_npcs.name: Headman Kanbei
key_npcs.role: Village head, party to the rice-debt arrangement with the estate
key_npcs.function: Hidden antagonist / source of complicity. Quietly let the kami-story stand because the truth implicates him and the estate in nearly selling a child. The social obstacle of Act IV.

key_npcs.name: Oharu (the real Tama)
key_npcs.role: The 'spirited-away' child, alive and grown, working in a down-valley post-town
key_npcs.function: Mystery payoff & living proof. Her existence detonates the red herring; reuniting/reconciling her with the valley is the climax. Gameplay-wise she's the destination of the late-game expedition lines.

key_npcs.name: The peddler Sōkichi
key_npcs.role: Itinerant medicine-seller who travels the whole region
key_npcs.function: Quest-giver & information broker. Trades rumor, rare goods, and the trail of clues that links down-valley; the practical engine that lets the player chase the truth beyond the home village (and a grounded source of many 'yokai' tales).

candidate_spines.emotional_hook: The ache of being handed an identity you didn't earn and aren't sure you want. A whole valley needs you to be their lost child — their grief, guilt, and hope are pinned to your face — and the kindest people (Sayo especially) will be hurt by the truth. So the player feels two pulls at once: the human longing to belong, to BE the miracle everyone wants; and the harder dignity of becoming someone real by your own hands instead. The growth-through-toil loop makes that dignity tangible — every callus is proof you are building a true self rather than wearing a borrowed one. Underneath it all: compassion for why people cling to comforting lies, and the quiet triumph of choosing an honest, hard-won name over a sweet false one.
candidate_spines.how_it_paces_unlocks: Each act gates the next tier of UI behind a story-and-effort milestone, so the incremental reveal feels diegetic. Act I keeps the screen near-empty (body/rest bar, rice counter, one task) because the character is literally bedridden — recovery IS the tutorial, justifying minimal UI. Surviving the first harvest unlocks the core labor panels (farm/forage/woodcut/fish) and the rice/koku economy. The humbling bandit defeat is the gate that unlocks the training and idle-combat panels — you only get them after the story proves you NEED them, so the player wants the new buttons rather than being dumped into them. Rising reputation (earned purely by sustained labor and survived seasons) is the key that unlocks NPC confessions, expedition routes, and craft tiers — i.e. mystery progress is literally bought with grind, marrying story curiosity to the numbers loop. Mountain-expedition and higher crafting tiers open as you investigate the 'yokai,' tying exploration unlocks to debunking superstitions. The Act V time-skip resets the loop one tier higher, re-paced for a larger map — giving the incremental UI a fresh, larger canvas to bloom across again.
risks: Tone slippage into magic: the residual-ambiguity omens (the guiding fox, the too-neat shrine sign) could read as confirmed supernatural if overwritten. Mitigation: keep them rare, off-screen, and always with a plausible mundane reading; never let them affect mechanics or grant anything.

Mistaken-identity reveal can feel like a cheat ('all that for nothing') if mishandled. Mitigation: plant fair, re-readable clues (age/gender drift in the legend, the porter's-knot habit, the debt-token) so the twist feels earned, and make the protagonist's chosen-name resolution emotionally satisfying regardless.

Heavy themes (child nearly sold for debt, abuse, smuggling, a stepfather's violence) must stay tasteful for the tone and audience. Mitigation: handle off-screen, with restraint and compassion, focused on consequences and mercy rather than spectacle.

Grind/curiosity mismatch: if mystery reveals are gated too hard behind reputation, players may stall and lose the thread. Mitigation: drip small story fragments on a steady cadence (sensory memory flashes, peddler rumors) between the big reputation-gated confessions so curiosity never goes cold.

Accidentally making the protagonist special: writers may be tempted to let a 'memory' restore real skill. Mitigation: enforce the rule that every returning fragment is ordinary labor/fear only, and every combat gain is traceable to on-screen training — review dialogue against the 'no hidden talent' constraint.

The real Tama being alive risks a tidy, sentimental ending that undercuts the grounded melancholy. Mitigation: make the reunion costly and incomplete (Oharu may not fully forgive the valley; some adults are never absolved), keeping it human rather than fairy-tale.

Cast bloat: many NPCs each carry a yokai-debunk + a mystery clue. Mitigation: ensure each NPC's grounded reveal and their mystery clue are the SAME beat where possible (Genza, Oyoné, Obaba Toki all do double duty) so the web stays legible.
